name: Home
title: Home
description: Landing page that lists featured and latest cybersecurity articles, exposes global search, provides navigation to key categories and series, and allows the single user to log in.

core_features: Load main navigation categories from getCategoriesForNavigation and render header links to category.html with the appropriate categoryId URL parameter.
Provide a global search form that, on submit, navigates to search.html with a query URL parameter based on the user-entered search term.
Retrieve featured and latest articles via getHomePageContent and render them as clickable article cards that navigate to article.html with articleId URL parameters.
Indicate article bookmark state (isBookmarkedInReadingList) and reading status (readingStatus) on article cards using the data returned from getHomePageContent.
Retrieve a list of popular series from getPopularSeriesForHome and render cards that navigate to series_detail.html with seriesId URL parameters.
Expose a login form that calls the login interface, updates in-page authenticated state, and gives feedback on success or failure without using URL parameters.
Provide footer navigation links to reading_list.html, settings.html, about.html, contact.html, privacy.html, and terms.html using direct links without additional parameters.
user_workflows: Browse featured and latest content: 1) User accesses index.html (no URL parameters). 2) Page calls getCategoriesForNavigation to populate the header navigation. 3) Page calls getHomePageContent to retrieve featuredArticles and latestArticles, including bookmark and reading status flags. 4) Page calls getPopularSeriesForHome to retrieve popular series. 5) User scrolls through featured and latest article cards. 6) User clicks an article card, triggering navigation to article.html with the articleId of the selected article in the URL.

Navigate by category from the header: 1) User loads index.html. 2) Header navigation is populated using getCategoriesForNavigation. 3) User clicks a category link in the header (e.g., a category whose id is 'tools'). 4) Browser navigates to category.html with categoryId set to the clicked category's id (e.g., category.html?categoryId=tools).

Start a search from the homepage: 1) User focuses the global search input on index.html. 2) User types a search term. 3) On form submit, client-side logic builds the URL search.html?query={encodedSearchTerm}. 4) Browser navigates to search.html with the query parameter; index.html does not process the query itself.

Open a popular series from the homepage: 1) User loads index.html. 2) Page calls getPopularSeriesForHome and renders series cards. 3) User clicks a series card. 4) Browser navigates to series_detail.html with seriesId set to the id of the clicked series (series_detail.html?seriesId={seriesId}).

Log in from the homepage: 1) User clicks a Login entry in the header or a dedicated login trigger. 2) Login component shows a username and password form. 3) User fills in credentials and submits the form. 4) Page calls login(username, password). 5) If the response indicates success and isAuthenticated is true, in-page authenticated state is updated and the login component shows a success state (e.g., hides form and shows that the user is logged in). 6) If success is false, the login component displays the message from the API and keeps the form visible so the user can retry.

Access other site sections from the homepage footer: 1) User scrolls to the footer on index.html. 2) User clicks one of the footer links. 3) Browser navigates to the target page: reading_list.html, settings.html, about.html, contact.html, privacy.html, or terms.html, without additional URL parameters.

Inspect reading list and reading status indicators on article cards: 1) User loads index.html. 2) getHomePageContent returns, per article, isBookmarkedInReadingList and readingStatus. 3) Home page displays indicators (e.g., bookmark icon state and reading status label) based solely on these returned values. 4) If the user clicks an article card, navigation goes to article.html?articleId={articleId}; the Home page does not modify bookmark or reading status itself.
interactions: Click on a category link in the header to navigate to category.html with a categoryId URL parameter equal to the clicked Category.id.
Type into the global search input and submit the search form to navigate to search.html with query set to the entered term.
Click on a featured article card to navigate to article.html with articleId set to the selected article's articleId.
Click on a latest article card to navigate to article.html with articleId set to the selected article's articleId.
Click on a popular series card to navigate to series_detail.html with seriesId set to the selected Series.id.
Click on the Login control to open the login component and display the login form.
Submit the login form to call login with the entered username and password, then update the UI based on the returned success, isAuthenticated, and message values.
Click on footer links such as Reading List, Settings, About, Contact, Privacy Policy, and Terms of Use to navigate to their respective pages using direct URLs without additional parameters.
Scroll the page vertically to move between featured content, latest content, and popular series sections; scrolling does not trigger additional API calls or change URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page (index.html) is accessed without required URL parameters and treats the absence of parameters as the default state. No incoming URL parameters are processed on this page; all content is derived from the assigned interfaces. Internal view state (e.g., whether the login form is visible or whether the user is considered authenticated for this session) is managed in memory and not encoded in the URL or stored in localStorage. URL parameters are used only when navigating away from the Home page: category links construct URLs like category.html?categoryId={categoryId}; the global search form constructs search.html?query={encodedSearchTerm}; article cards construct article.html?articleId={articleId}; series cards construct series_detail.html?seriesId={seriesId}. Footer links navigate to static targets (e.g., reading_list.html) without parameters. The Home page does not modify reading_status or bookmark data and does not write any user-specific state through URL parameters.

components.functionality: Fetches categories using getCategoriesForNavigation and renders them as primary navigation links to category.html with appropriate categoryId URL parameters, along with a link to series.html and a Home link to index.html.
event_handlers: onInit: call getCategoriesForNavigation() and store returned categories for rendering

components.functionality: Allows the user to enter a free-text search query and, on submission, navigates to search.html with the query parameter set to the entered term.
data_binding: URL query parameter: query (when navigating to search.html)
event_handlers: onChangeInput: update local searchTerm state from the input field
onSubmit: prevent default form submission, URL-encode searchTerm, and navigate to search.html?query={encodedSearchTerm}

components.functionality: Displays a list of featured article cards using the featuredArticles array from getHomePageContent, including category information, summary, meta data, bookmark state, and reading status.
event_handlers: onInit: call getHomePageContent() and store featuredArticles for this section

components.functionality: Displays a list of latest article cards using the latestArticles array from getHomePageContent, with the same metadata and indicators as the featured section.
event_handlers: onInit: call getHomePageContent() if not already called, or reuse previously fetched latestArticles

components.functionality: Reusable card component used by both featured-articles-section and latest-articles-section to render a single article's title, summary, category, published date, reading time, upvotes, average rating, comment count, difficulty, tags, bookmark state, and reading status.
data_binding: Article (as provided in getHomePageContent.featuredArticles and getHomePageContent.latestArticles)

components.functionality: Displays a concise list or strip of popular learning series using data from getPopularSeriesForHome, each leading to its detail page.
event_handlers: onInit: call getPopularSeriesForHome() and store returned series list

components.functionality: Provides a username/password form for the single user, calls login on submit, and updates in-page authentication state and error/success messaging based on the response.
event_handlers: onOpen: set panel visibility to true when triggered from main-navigation-header
onChangeUsername: update local username state from input
onChangePassword: update local password state from input
onSubmit: call login(username, password), then if response.success and response.isAuthenticated are true, set authenticated state and hide login form; otherwise, display response.message as an error
onClose: hide the login panel without changing URL parameters

components.functionality: Renders links to key auxiliary pages such as Reading List, Settings, About, Contact, Privacy Policy, and Terms of Use using direct navigation.
data_binding: Static routes: reading_list.html, settings.html, about.html, contact.html, privacy.html, terms.html

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid similar to Bootstrap: a full-width container for the hero and navigation, then a centered content container with a ~3/9 or 4/8 column split between sidebar and main content.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom flow: a fixed, full-width navigation bar at the very top; directly beneath it, a full-width dark hero band containing the site identity, a large left-aligned headline, and the global search form. Below the hero, a centered main content container creates a two-column layout: a narrower left sidebar (housing the login panel and any author/meta content) and a wider right column that stacks, in order, the Featured Articles section, a horizontal Popular Series strip, and then the Latest Articles section (both composed of repeated article cards). A full-width footer navigation anchors the bottom of the page.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom flow: a fixed, full-width navigation bar at the very top; directly beneath it, a full-width dark hero band containing the site identity, a large left-aligned headline, and the global search form. Below the hero, a centered main content container creates a two-column layout: a narrower left sidebar (housing the login panel and any author/meta content) and a wider right column that stacks, in order, the Featured Articles section, a horizontal Popular Series strip, and then the Latest Articles section (both composed of repeated article cards). A full-width footer navigation anchors the bottom of the page.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The navigation and hero together occupy roughly the top 25–30% of the initial viewport height, both spanning 100% width. The main content sits in a centered container with wide left/right gutters at large desktop sizes (~10–15% page width each), and a max content width of ~1100–1200px. Within this container, the left sidebar uses about 25–30% of the width (≈3–4 columns of a 12-column grid), and the main content column uses 70–75% (≈8–9 columns). Vertical spacing between major sections (hero → main container, featured → series strip → latest) is around 32–48px, with 16–24px between related elements and 8–16px between text elements inside components.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is moderately spacious and editorial: generous line height, no crammed elements, and clear separation between blocks via whitespace rather than heavy borders. Cards and panels have 16–24px internal padding and 24–32px external margins where sections change. Lists of article cards are comfortably spaced, making scanning easy without feeling sparse. Visual hierarchy is driven by typography, spacing, and subtle dividers rather than strong shadows.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the top-left logo in the fixed navigation, moves horizontally across the main nav links (Z-pattern), then diagonally down into the hero’s left-aligned headline and global search. From there, the eye continues down into the main content area, first catching the right-column section heading (Featured Articles), then moving downward through the list of article cards (F-pattern down the main column). The left sidebar with the login panel is a secondary focal path: it’s immediately visible but visually quieter, drawing periodic glances as users scroll the main content.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a slim, fixed navigation bar spanning the full width of the viewport, with the 'Jack Hacks' brand lockup aligned to the left and a compact row of navigation links aligned to the right. Directly beneath, a dark, moody hero section stretches edge to edge, occupying about a quarter of the initial viewport height. On the left side of this hero, a prominent headline introduces the cybersecurity blog, with a short supporting line below; directly under this copy, the global search form sits as a wide, clean search bar, reinforcing search as a primary action. Below the hero, a generous band of whitespace leads into a centered main content container whose width is visually framed by large left and right gutters. Inside this container, the layout splits into two columns: on the left, a narrower sidebar column holds a compact login panel card near the top; on the right, a broader main column first introduces a 'Featured Articles' block, then a horizontally laid-out 'Popular Series' strip, followed by a taller 'Latest Articles' list. Each article appears as a simple, full-width text card with a bold title and short excerpt, vertically stacked with consistent spacing. The overall feeling is clean and modern: thin dividers, large headings, and carefully tuned whitespace guide the eye smoothly downward through stories while the sidebar quietly supports secondary tasks. At the very bottom, a restrained footer navigation spans the width of the page, with centered or evenly spaced links, visually separated from the main content by a comfortable vertical gap.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main navigation header is a fixed horizontal bar anchored at the very top of the viewport, spanning 100% of the page width. It has a modest height (approximately 64px) and sits on a flat, slightly darker background than the page body to distinguish it without heavy shadows. Within this bar, content is centered in the same max-width container used for the main content below (≈1100–1200px), leaving generous left and right gutters on very wide screens. The 'Jack Hacks' logo or wordmark is aligned to the left, occupying roughly the left 20–25% of the container, while a horizontal row of navigation links (e.g., Home, Categories, About, Contact, Login) is aligned to the right. Links are evenly spaced with 16–24px between each. The bar stays fixed as the user scrolls, so the rest of the content (hero, main column, sidebar, footer) scrolls underneath it. A small amount of padding (12–16px) on top and bottom keeps the elements vertically centered and consistent with the 8px spacing system.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the top of the viewport (position: fixed, top: 0, full-width), remaining visible as the user scrolls; other page sections begin immediately below it, accounting for its height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search form sits inside the full-width hero section, aligned to the left to mirror the mockup’s hero title alignment. Visually, it appears approximately in the lower-left quadrant of the hero: the hero’s main headline is at the top-left of the hero band, a short supporting line sits directly beneath it, and the search form is placed 16–24px below the supporting text. The search input is a prominent, horizontally oriented bar that spans roughly 50–60% of the hero’s inner content width (about 40–60% of the page width on a large desktop), capped at a comfortable max width (~480–600px). It is horizontally aligned with the left edge of the hero text block, not centered on the page, reinforcing the asymmetric, content-focused feel. The input field has 12–16px internal vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, with a subtle border or faint outline and no heavy shadow. A search icon or button is positioned on the right end of the field. There is ample space above (from the hero top to the headline) and below (from the search field to the hero bottom), preserving the spacious, low-density hero aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section; it scrolls away with the hero as the user moves down the page, remaining aligned to the left within the hero’s inner content container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured articles section is the first major block in the right-hand main column of the centered content container, directly below the hero and slightly lower than the top of the left sidebar’s login panel to preserve the right-column priority. It occupies the full width of the right column (approximately 70–75% of the container, or 8–9 grid columns). At the top of this section, a bold, left-aligned heading such as 'Featured Articles' sits flush with the right column’s left edge, with 0–8px margin above and 8–12px margin below. Beneath the heading, a thin, subtle horizontal divider (a 1px line with low-contrast color) spans roughly 60–80% of the right column width, aligned to the heading. Below this divider, featured article cards (instances of the article-card component) are stacked vertically with consistent spacing: each card spans the full width of the right column, and each is separated from the next by roughly 24px of vertical space. The section as a whole has 24–32px of top margin from the hero and 32–40px of bottom margin before the Popular Series strip, reinforcing its role as a distinct yet integrated block in the main content flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing with the document; it serves as the visual anchor at the top of the main content column, influencing scroll behavior by drawing the eye there after the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The latest articles section appears in the right-hand main column directly below the Popular Series strip, continuing the vertical flow of content. It also spans the full width of the right column (≈70–75% of the container). A section heading such as 'Latest Articles' or 'Recent Posts' is left-aligned to the same text baseline as the Featured Articles heading above, maintaining a consistent vertical rhythm. There is a slightly larger vertical gap (approximately 32–40px) from the bottom of the Popular Series strip to this heading to signal a new major section. Beneath the heading, a similar light horizontal divider runs 60–80% of the section width. Below that, a taller list of article cards is stacked vertically—more items than the featured section—each article-card occupying the full width of the column. Cards are separated by a moderate, consistent vertical margin (24–32px), providing a rhythm that supports scanning. The entire latest section extends further down the page than other sections, forming the primary scrollable reading area, while the left sidebar remains relatively shorter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrollable as part of the main content column; forms the main vertical reading spine of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each article card is a rectangular, full-width block that lives within either the Featured Articles or Latest Articles sections. It spans 100% of the width of the column it resides in, with internal padding of 16–24px on all sides. The card uses a very light background contrast or a subtle bottom border to differentiate it from the page background, avoiding heavy outlines or shadows. The content inside is arranged vertically: a bold, left-aligned article title at the top; 8–12px below it, a small meta line (e.g., date, category, reading time) in a lighter, smaller type; and 8–16px further down, a short 2–3 line excerpt with generous line height. Total vertical height varies based on excerpt length, but cards maintain consistent padding and spacing so they align harmoniously when stacked. The bottom of each card has 0 padding margin inside, but the card itself is separated from the next card with 24–32px of external bottom margin. On hover (desktop), the card may exhibit a subtle background tint or border-color shift to indicate interactivity without adding strong shadows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying full column width within its parent list; cards stack vertically and do not overlap or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The popular series strip is a horizontal band positioned in the right-hand main column between the Featured Articles section and the Latest Articles section. It spans the full width of the right column and uses a slightly tinted or subtly darker background than the main page to distinguish it as a separate but lightweight feature block. There is approximately 32px of vertical space between the bottom of the Featured Articles section and the top of this strip. Inside, the strip has 16–24px of padding on all sides. A small, left-aligned label like 'Popular Series' sits at the top of the strip, with 8–12px of space below it. Beneath the label, series items (e.g., pill-shaped buttons or compact cards) are arranged in a single horizontal row, flowing left to right. On wide screens, several series pills are visible side by side with 8–16px of horizontal gap between them; if there are more items than fit, they wrap onto a second line or can be horizontally scrollable within the strip. The overall height of the strip is modest (around 80–120px depending on content), acting as a low-profile horizontal scannable area that breaks up the vertical list of articles without overwhelming it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column, forming a horizontal interruption between vertical lists; on smaller screens it may become a horizontally scrollable row, but by default it flows within the normal document.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The login panel is placed in the left sidebar column at the top of the main content area, roughly aligned vertically with the top of the Featured Articles heading in the right column. It appears as a compact card spanning 100% of the sidebar width (≈25–30% of the total content container). The card has 16–24px padding on all sides and a subtle border or faint background contrast to distinguish it from the page body while remaining visually light. At the top of the panel, a concise heading such as 'Sign In' is left-aligned, followed by 8–12px of space and then the form fields stacked vertically: email/username field, 8–12px gap, password field, 16px gap, and a full-width primary button aligned to the left edge of the panel. Any helper text or link (e.g., 'Forgot password?') sits beneath the button with 8–12px of spacing. There is ~24–32px of margin between this panel and any additional sidebar content that may be placed below. Visually, it reads as a secondary utility card: easily accessible but less bold than the hero or main article content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left sidebar; it scrolls with the page and does not overlay or fix to the viewport. On smaller screens where columns stack, it moves above or below the main content as per responsive rules.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer navigation sits at the very bottom of the page, spanning the full width of the viewport and visually anchored after the end of the Latest Articles section. There is a generous vertical gap (32–48px) between the last article card and the footer, reinforcing it as a separate page region. The footer has a slightly darker or subtly tinted background than the main content area, but remains flat without heavy shadow. Inside, content is centered within the same max-width container as the rest of the page. Navigation links—such as 'Privacy', 'Terms', 'RSS', and social icons—are arranged either in a single centered row or left/right alignment depending on preference, with 16–24px of horizontal spacing between links. Vertical padding of 16–24px top and bottom gives the footer enough presence without making it heavy. A fine top border or subtle separation line can be used to differentiate it from the content above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the document, appearing after all main content; it does not stick or float, and comes into view as the user scrolls to the end of the page.

name: Category Listing
title: Category Listing
description: Displays a filterable, sortable list of articles in a single category, with tag filters and quick bookmarking to the default Reading List.

core_features: Read current URL parameters (categoryId, tag, dateFrom, dateTo, readingTimeMax, difficulty, minUpvotes, challengeYearFrom, challengeYearTo, sort, page) to determine which category listing and filters are active.
Call getCategoryFilterOptions(categoryId) to load available tags, difficulty options, reading time and upvote ranges, and sort options for the current category.
Render filter controls (tags, date range, reading time max, difficulty, min upvotes, challenge year range) based on getCategoryFilterOptions and the current URL parameters.
Render sorting controls (e.g., based on availableSortOptions from getCategoryFilterOptions) and bind them to the sort URL parameter.
Call getCategoryArticles(...) with parameters derived from the URL (and pageSize from application configuration) to display article cards for the current category and filter set.
Display article metadata on cards, including tags, reading time, upvotes, averageRating, commentCount, difficulty, challengeYear, isBookmarkedInReadingList, and readingStatus.
Allow navigation to article.html?articleId=... when an article title is clicked.
Allow tag-based filtering by navigating to category.html with the same categoryId and an updated tag parameter when a tag chip is clicked on a card or in the sidebar.
Allow the user to change filters and sorting, then navigate to category.html with updated query parameters (page reset to 1) to refresh the listing.
Allow bookmarking/unbookmarking directly from article cards by calling toggleBookmarkDefaultReadingList(articleId) and updating the card’s bookmark state using the returned isBookmarked value.
Provide pagination controls that update the page URL parameter and reload the listing with getCategoryArticles while preserving other filters.
user_workflows:
Browsing a category with filters:
1) User navigates to category.html with a categoryId parameter.
2) Page-controller parses URL parameters and calls getCategoryFilterOptions(categoryId).
3) When filter options are loaded, the filter-sidebar and sort-bar are populated with available choices and current selections from the URL.
4) Page-controller calls getCategoryArticles(...) using categoryId, tag (converted to tagSlugs), dateFrom, dateTo, readingTimeMax, difficulty, minUpvotes, challengeYearFrom, challengeYearTo, sort, and page from the URL.
5) Article-list renders article cards based on the articles array from getCategoryArticles.
6) User reviews the listing, changes filters or sorting, and submits the filter form or changes sort; the page navigates to category.html with updated query parameters and page=1.
7) Steps 2–5 repeat with the new parameters.

Filtering by tag from sidebar or card:
1) User sees tags in the filter-sidebar (from getCategoryFilterOptions.availableTags) or on article cards (from getCategoryArticles.articles[].tags).
2) User clicks a tag.
3) Tag-click handler builds a new URL for category.html with the same categoryId and the tag parameter set according to the clicked tag slug (and optionally updated to include or replace other tags according to the selection mode).
4) Browser navigates to the new URL.
5) Page reloads: page-controller parses the new tag value, converts it into tagSlugs for getCategoryArticles, and re-renders the listing with the tag filter active.

Opening an article from the listing:
1) User clicks the title of an article card.
2) Article-card constructs a link to article.html with articleId set to the card’s articleId.
3) Browser navigates to article.html?articleId=... where the article is displayed on the article detail page.

Quick bookmarking from listing:
1) User sees the bookmark icon on each article card, whose visual state reflects isBookmarkedInReadingList from getCategoryArticles.
2) User clicks the bookmark icon on a specific card.
3) Article-card calls toggleBookmarkDefaultReadingList(articleId) for that article.
4) On success, the card updates its bookmark state to the returned isBookmarked value and may trigger a brief confirmation message using the response (success, message, collectionName, totalItemsInCollection).
5) The page does not reload; only the single card’s bookmark state changes.

Adjusting pagination:
1) User scrolls to the bottom of the listing and sees pagination-controls showing current page and available pages derived from totalCount and pageSize returned by getCategoryArticles.
2) User clicks on a different page number or next/previous.
3) Pagination handler builds a new URL for category.html with the same categoryId and all existing filter and sort parameters, but with the page parameter set to the selected page.
4) Browser navigates to this URL.
5) Page reloads, page-controller calls getCategoryArticles with the new page value, and article-list renders the corresponding subset of articles.

Using global search from a category page:
1) User enters text into the global search field in the header.
2) User submits the search form.
3) Header-search-form navigates to search.html with query set to the entered term.
4) Category page is left; search.html handles displaying the search results.
interactions: On initial page load or URL change, parse all supported query parameters and call getCategoryFilterOptions(categoryId) followed by getCategoryArticles(...) with arguments derived from those parameters.

On change of any filter control (tag selection, dateFrom/dateTo, readingTimeMax, difficulty, minUpvotes, challengeYearFrom, challengeYearTo) and submission of the filter form, rebuild the category.html URL with updated parameters and page=1, then navigate to that URL.
On change of the sort control, immediately rebuild the category.html URL with the updated sort value and page=1 (preserving other filter parameters), then navigate to that URL.
On click of a tag in the sidebar or on an article card, rebuild the category.html URL with the same categoryId and tag set (or updated) to reflect the clicked tag’s slug, then navigate to that URL.
On click of an article title, navigate to article.html with articleId set to the clicked article’s identifier.

On click of the bookmark icon on a card, call toggleBookmarkDefaultReadingList(articleId); after receiving the response, update the card’s bookmark visual state based on isBookmarked and optionally show a transient confirmation message.
On click of pagination controls (specific page, next, previous), rebuild the category.html URL with the selected page value and all existing filter and sort parameters, then navigate to that URL.
On submission of the global search form in the header, navigate to search.html with query set to the submitted search term.
On click of the site logo or Home navigation item, navigate to index.html (leaving the category page).
On selection of 'Reading List' from the user profile menu, navigate to reading_list.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The category listing state is fully derived from URL query parameters. On load, the page reads categoryId (required) and optional tag, dateFrom, dateTo, readingTimeMax, difficulty, minUpvotes, challengeYearFrom, challengeYearTo, sort, and page from the querystring. These values are stored in in-memory state for the current render only and are not persisted outside the URL. The page first calls getCategoryFilterOptions(categoryId) to obtain availableTags, difficultyOptions, readingTimeRange, upvoteRange, and availableSortOptions, which drive the configuration and valid ranges of the filter and sort controls. Next, the page constructs the arguments for getCategoryArticles: categoryId is passed directly; tag is parsed into a tagSlugs collection (for example, a single slug or a parsed list from a delimited string representation in the tag parameter); dateFrom, dateTo, readingTimeMax, difficulty, minUpvotes, challengeYearFrom, challengeYearTo, and sort are passed as-is from the URL when present; page is taken from the URL or a default page value if missing; pageSize is determined by application configuration rather than the URL. The response from getCategoryArticles supplies totalCount, page, pageSize, and an articles array; these values are used to render article cards and to compute pagination controls. Any interaction that changes filter, sort, or pagination state results in constructing a new URL for category.html with updated query parameters (including page=1 when filters or sort are changed) and navigating to that URL, causing the cycle of parsing parameters and calling interfaces to repeat. The bookmark state for each article card is initially taken from articles[].isBookmarkedInReadingList and can be updated in-place when toggleBookmarkDefaultReadingList returns, without altering the URL. No state is stored in localStorage or other persistent client-side mechanisms; the URL is the single source of truth for the listing configuration.

components.functionality: Coordinates the entire category listing page lifecycle: parses URL parameters, triggers data loading via getCategoryFilterOptions and getCategoryArticles, supplies data and configuration to child components, and reacts to child events by updating the URL to represent the new state.

Displays the current category label and a concise summary of active filters (such as active tag or date range) using values from the parsed URL parameters and, when available, article metadata (e.g., categoryName from the first article). Provides context for what subset of content is being shown.

Renders and manages filters for the category listing, including tag selection, dateFrom/dateTo inputs, readingTimeMax slider or input, difficulty selector (where applicable), minUpvotes control, and challengeYearFrom/challengeYearTo controls. Initializes controls based on getCategoryFilterOptions and current URL parameters. On form submission, emits a filters change event with the updated filter values.

Displays the sorting dropdown using availableSortOptions from getCategoryFilterOptions and highlights the current sort selection from the URL. Also displays active filter chips (e.g., active tag, date range, reading time limit) derived from current URL parameters and allows the user to clear specific filters by clicking on chips. Changing the sort or clearing a filter emits events used by the page-controller to rebuild the URL.

Displays the list of article cards based on the articles array returned from getCategoryArticles. Handles the empty-state scenario when totalCount is zero by showing a functional 'no results' message and optionally suggesting that the user adjust filters (using only dynamic values, not static examples). Delegates tag clicks and bookmark interactions to article-card components and aggregates their events for the page-controller.

Represents a single article in the listing. Shows title, summary, publishedAt, readingTimeMinutes, upvotes, averageRating, commentCount, difficulty, challengeYear (if present), readingStatus, and associated tags. Indicates whether the article is currently bookmarked using isBookmarkedInReadingList. Supports clicking the title to navigate to article.html, clicking tags to trigger tag filtering, and clicking the bookmark icon to toggle membership in the default Reading List via toggleBookmarkDefaultReadingList.

Displays pagination elements based on totalCount, page, and pageSize from getCategoryArticles. Allows the user to navigate between pages while preserving the current filters and sorting. Emits a page change event with the selected page number when the user interacts with next/previous or specific page links.

Provides a global search input in the site header. When the form is submitted, it navigates away from the category page to search.html with query set to the entered search term, without modifying or relying on the category-specific interfaces.

Listens for successful responses from toggleBookmarkDefaultReadingList and shows a short-lived confirmation message using the returned message and collectionName/totalItemsInCollection. Handles both add and remove cases, and dismisses itself automatically after a timeout or when manually closed.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid similar to Bootstrap: a full-width container for the hero and navigation, then a centered content container with a ~3/9 or 4/8 column split between sidebar and main content.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear flow: a fixed horizontal navigation bar at the very top (containing the logo on the left and the header search form on the right), followed immediately by a full-width hero/category header section with a dark, moody background. Below the hero, a centered main content container uses a two-column layout: a narrower left column for the filter sidebar and a wider right column for the sort controls, active filters, article list, and pagination. Bookmark feedback appears as a floating toast overlay on top of this structure, without affecting layout flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear flow: a fixed horizontal navigation bar at the very top (containing the logo on the left and the header search form on the right), followed immediately by a full-width hero/category header section with a dark, moody background. Below the hero, a centered main content container uses a two-column layout: a narrower left column for the filter sidebar and a wider right column for the sort controls, active filters, article list, and pagination. Bookmark feedback appears as a floating toast overlay on top of this structure, without affecting layout flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero/category header uses roughly the top 25–30% of the initial viewport height and spans 100% width. Below it, the main content is constrained to a centered max-width container (approximately 1100–1200px) with generous left/right gutters (~10–15% of viewport on large screens). Within this container, the filter sidebar occupies about 25–30% of the width (3–4 columns of a 12-column grid), while the main article column occupies the remaining 70–75% (8–9 columns). Vertical spacing between major sections (hero → main content, sort bar → article list, article list → pagination) follows the 32–48px scale, while spacing between individual items (article cards, filter blocks) follows the 16–24px scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps a moderately spacious editorial feel. Text lines in headings, summaries, and article cards use comfortable line-height with 8–16px gaps between text elements. Components are clearly separated using whitespace rather than strong borders. Filter options and article cards each sit in lightly bounded blocks with 16–24px internal padding. The density supports scanning and reading, avoiding cramped sidebars or overly condensed article grids.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the Z-pattern from the mockup: the eye starts at the top left with the logo, sweeps right across the navigation and header search, then diagonally down to the prominent left-aligned category title in the hero. From there, the eye continues down into the main content container where the first focal point is the sort-and-active-filters bar at the top of the right column, then flows vertically down the article list (forming an F-pattern for readers). The left filter sidebar is a secondary attraction, catching the eye at key scroll positions, while the bookmark feedback toast briefly interrupts attention at the lower-right corner when triggered, without overpowering the main reading flow.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a clean, modern cybersecurity blog category view. A fixed, slim navigation bar pins to the very top, stretching full width with the logo on the left and a compact, right-aligned search field. Directly beneath, a dark, full-width hero section introduces the category: on the left, a large, bold category title and short description sit atop a subtly moody background, perhaps with faint code or network motifs; on the right, extra details like article counts or last updated date are aligned but visually subdued. Below this hero, the layout shifts into a centered, max-width content column with wide side gutters. Inside, the page splits into two columns: on the left, a tidy filter sidebar stacked from top to bottom with tag filters and controls, each block separated by comfortable white space; on the right, the main column begins with a horizontal sort-and-active-filters bar, then a vertically stacked list of article cards, each given room to breathe. Pagination sits at the bottom of the main column, centered and clearly separated from the last article. Throughout, the design stays flat and minimal, with light lines and subtle dividers at most. When a user bookmarks an article, a small, dark, rounded toast gently appears in the lower-right corner above the content, confirming the action without disrupting the overall quiet, reading-focused layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category-page-controller is the overarching layout frame that spans the full browser viewport horizontally and vertically. It positions the global navigation bar fixed at the very top and ensures that the hero section and the centered two-column main content scroll beneath it. It defines a full-width background canvas, within which it centers the main content container below the hero to a max width of roughly 1100–1200px, leaving broad left and right gutters on large screens and reducing those gutters proportionally on smaller screens. All other page components (hero/category header, filter sidebar, sort bar, article list, pagination, and bookmark toast) are placed within this controller’s flow, aligning to its vertical rhythm and spacing rules (32–48px between major blocks, 16–24px within blocks).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the primary layout wrapper; scrollable vertical page container with a fixed navigation region at the top and all other sections positioned in normal document flow within it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category-header-summary occupies the full-width hero area directly beneath the fixed navigation bar. It extends edge-to-edge horizontally and roughly 25–30% of the initial viewport height vertically. The background is a dark, slightly textured or photographic panel that conveys a moody cybersecurity feel. Content is aligned within a centered inner container that respects the same max width as the main content; within this container, the category title is left-aligned and placed in the upper-left quadrant of the hero, using a large, bold type size. Below the title, a short category description or tagline appears with 8–16px spacing, followed by compact metadata such as the number of articles or last updated date. Any supplementary stats or category labels can be right-aligned in the same inner container but kept visually subordinate so the main title remains the focal point. There is generous vertical padding (24–48px) above and below the text to maintain the spacious, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow, spanning full width; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page while remaining visually anchored directly beneath the fixed navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-sidebar sits in the left column of the main content area, directly below the hero and aligned with the top of the sort-and-active-filters bar in the right column. Within the centered main container, it occupies roughly 25–30% of the usable width (equivalent to 3–4 columns on a 12-column grid). It is composed of vertically stacked filter sections—such as tag checkboxes, difficulty levels, and date ranges—each presented as a light card or panel with 16–24px internal padding. Individual filter groups are separated by 24–32px of vertical whitespace to avoid clutter. The overall column maintains a clear left edge aligned with the container, while its right edge aligns with the visual gutter separating it from the main article column. On taller screens, the top portion of the sidebar remains visible as the user scrolls, subtly encouraging interaction without overshadowing the content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned in the normal flow within the left column; may use a sticky behavior so that its top edge pins just below the navigation + hero boundary when scrolling, remaining visible within its column until the bottom of the main content is reached.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-and-active-filters bar is anchored at the top of the right main column, directly opposite the upper portion of the filter sidebar. It spans the full width of the right column (about 70–75% of the container) and appears immediately below the transition from the hero into the main content. Vertically, it has 16–24px of internal padding and sits with approximately 24–32px of whitespace above the first article card, clearly separating controls from content. On the left side of this bar, the current category label or a simple 'Articles in [Category]' text and an article count can be placed, aligned to echo the 'Recent Posts' label in the original mockup. On the right side, sorting controls (e.g., a dropdown for 'Newest', 'Most Popular') and view toggles are right-aligned. Below these controls, active filter chips appear on a new row spanning the bar’s width, wrapping to multiple lines when necessary, with 8px vertical gaps. A subtle bottom border or hairline divider visually anchors this bar and signals that the list of articles begins just below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column’s flow; it scrolls with the content. On larger screens, it visually reads as the header for the article list section, providing a consistent anchor before the user scrolls into the articles.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-list occupies the main portion of the right column beneath the sort-and-active-filters bar. It spans the full width of that column (approximately 70–75% of the total content width) and is arranged as a single-column vertical stack of article cards, echoing the mockup’s editorial list style rather than a tight grid. The first article card begins roughly 24–32px below the bottom of the sort-and-active-filters bar. Each subsequent card is separated by 24–32px of vertical whitespace to keep the list readable and scannable. The left edge of the article list aligns cleanly with the left edge of the sort-and-active-filters bar, reinforcing a strong vertical line. On wider displays, the list occupies the right two-thirds of the central content band, with the filter sidebar maintaining a parallel column to its left. On smaller screens, when the layout collapses, the article list transitions to full width beneath the filters while preserving vertical spacing and single-column stacking.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; scrollable with the main content. It behaves as a flexible vertical container that grows based on the number of article cards, pushing pagination controls downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each article-card is a rectangular content block within the article-list, spanning nearly the full width of the right column while respecting internal padding and outer margins. The card uses a light, flat background with subtle or no border; if a border is used, it is a 1px, low-contrast line. There is 16–24px of internal padding on all sides. At the top of the card, the article title appears left-aligned in a bold, slightly larger font, with 8–12px spacing below it. Underneath, a short excerpt (2–3 lines) provides a quick overview, separated from the title by comfortable line spacing. Additional metadata (tags, read time, publish date) is displayed in a smaller, muted style either at the bottom left of the card or in a compact row just below the excerpt with 8px separation. A bookmark icon or button is placed consistently—either top right or bottom right—using minimal visual weight. Vertical spacing between cards is 24–32px, allowing each to read as a distinct unit, similar to the post previews in the original mockup’s main column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static elements stacked inside the article-list container; they do not float or fix, but can react to hover states or bookmarking interactions without affecting the surrounding layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls sit directly below the list of article cards in the right column, occupying the same width as the article-list (about 70–75% of the content width) and centered horizontally within that column. There is a clear separation of about 32–40px between the last article card and the pagination row, giving a visual sense of the list ending before navigation options appear. The controls themselves are compact: page numbers, 'Previous' and 'Next' arrows, and possibly a dropdown for items per page are laid out in a single horizontal line or in two aligned rows. The main pagination elements are center-aligned within the right column, maintaining the balanced, editorial feel. A subtle top border or faint shadow line can be used to distinguish the pagination from the article list without adding heavy visual weight.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column’s flow; it remains anchored at the bottom of the article-list area and moves down as more articles are loaded or as the viewport changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-search-form resides within the fixed top navigation bar, aligned to the right side. The nav bar spans the full width of the viewport and has a modest height, with roughly 16px vertical padding. The logo and primary navigation links occupy the left and center portions, while the search form is tucked into the rightmost third. The search input itself is a horizontally oriented field occupying about 30–40% of the nav bar’s internal width on desktop, but visually it appears compact due to minimal borders and flat styling. There is 8–16px of horizontal padding inside the field and clear 8–12px spacing between it and any adjacent icons or user avatar. The search form aligns vertically with the nav bar’s centerline, ensuring a clean, continuous top edge above the hero section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position indirectly, as it is embedded within the fixed navigation bar at the top of the viewport. It remains constantly visible as users scroll through the hero and main content below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmark-feedback-toast is a small, unobtrusive notification that overlays the main content area without shifting layout. It appears in the lower-right corner of the viewport, hovering above both the sidebar and the article column. Horizontally, it sits about 24–32px from the right edge of the viewport; vertically, it is positioned about 24–32px above the bottom edge, clear of any fixed browser UI. The toast has a compact, rectangular shape with slightly rounded corners, and a dark background that echoes the hero’s moody tones but with enough contrast to stand out against the page content. Inside, text is left-aligned with 12–16px padding on all sides, and any icon (such as a checkmark or bookmark symbol) is placed to the left of the text with 8px spacing. Only one toast is visible at a time; it auto-dismisses after a few seconds, with vertical stacking or subtle offset if multiple toasts trigger in quick succession.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed relative to the viewport, overlaying above all scrollable content. It does not affect page layout and appears/disappears temporarily in response to bookmark interactions.

name: Search Results
title: Search Results
description: Page that displays and refines article search results based on a text query, with filters for content type and topic tags, sorting options, pagination, and quick bookmarking to the default Reading List.

core_features: Display a paginated list of article search results for the current query using searchArticles, including metadata such as category, tags, rating, comments, and reading status.
Load available content type filter options, topic tags, and sort options using getSearchFilterOptions and bind them to filter controls and dropdowns.
Filter results by one or more content types and topic tags, keeping filter state in the URL as contentTypes and tagSlugs parameters.
Sort results by relevance, newest, most commented, or highest rated, using the sort URL parameter and getSearchFilterOptions.availableSortOptions.
Allow users to open article detail pages (article.html) from result cards, including support for opening in new tabs via standard browser behavior.
Provide quick bookmarking and unbookmarking of articles to the default Reading List directly from search result cards using toggleBookmarkDefaultReadingList, and reflect bookmark state in the UI.
Support navigation to category-specific listing pages (category.html) by clicking category labels on result cards.
Offer pagination controls that update the page URL parameter and reload results accordingly via searchArticles.
user_workflows: Run a new search from the header: 1) The user types a query into the global header search field. 2) The user submits the form, which navigates to search.html with query set to the entered text and without adding contentTypes, tagSlugs, sort, or page parameters (or resetting them to default). 3) On page load, the controller component reads the query parameter, calls getSearchFilterOptions to load filters and sort options, and calls searchArticles with the query and default or URL-derived filter and sort values. 4) The results list and related components render based on the searchArticles response.

Refine results by content type and topic tags: 1) On initial load, the sidebar filter component calls getSearchFilterOptions (via the page controller) and displays content type and topic tag options with selection state derived from the contentTypes and tagSlugs URL parameters. 2) The user selects or deselects one or more content type checkboxes and topic tag checkboxes. 3) The user clicks an Apply Filters control, which constructs a navigation to search.html with the same query, updated contentTypes and tagSlugs values, the current or default sort, and page set to 1. 4) The page reloads, the controller re-reads the URL, and searchArticles is called with the new filter values; the results list updates accordingly. 5) The active filter chips bar reflects the newly selected filters.

Change sort order of results: 1) The sort control above the results is initialized from getSearchFilterOptions.availableSortOptions and the sort URL parameter (or a default if missing). 2) The user selects a different sort option from the dropdown. 3) The sort control triggers navigation to search.html with the same query, existing contentTypes and tagSlugs values, updated sort, and page set to 1. 4) The controller re-runs searchArticles with the new sort parameter; the results list is re-rendered in the new order.

Bookmark or unbookmark an article from the results: 1) Each result card displays a bookmark icon whose state is bound to the isBookmarkedInReadingList field from searchArticles. 2) When the user clicks the bookmark icon, the card triggers toggleBookmarkDefaultReadingList with the articleId of that result. 3) On successful response, the card updates its bookmark icon state based on the returned isBookmarked value and optionally displays a non-blocking toast message using the message and collectionName from the response. 4) The user can later navigate to reading_list.html (via header navigation) to see the updated Reading List; this page is reached without additional interfaces from the search page.

Use pagination to browse more results: 1) The pagination component receives totalCount, page, and pageSize from the searchArticles response and computes how many pages of results exist. 2) The user clicks a page number, previous, or next control. 3) The pagination component triggers navigation to search.html with the same query, contentTypes, tagSlugs, sort values, and page set to the selected page number. 4) On reload, the controller calls searchArticles with the new page value; only results for that page are shown.

Filter by clicking tags or category labels on a result card: 1) The user clicks a category label on a result card; the browser navigates to category.html with categoryId equal to the card's categoryId. 2) Alternatively, the user clicks a tag chip rendered on the result card. 3) The tag click triggers navigation to search.html with query preserved, contentTypes preserved, tagSlugs updated to include the clicked tag's slug (or set to that single tag if the implementation chooses), sort preserved, and page set to 1. 4) The page reloads, searchArticles is called with the updated tagSlugs, and results are filtered to that tag context.

Clear or adjust active filters from the active-filters bar: 1) The active-filters bar displays chips for each selected content type and tag based on the contentTypes and tagSlugs URL parameters combined with labels from getSearchFilterOptions. 2) The user clicks the remove icon on a filter chip. 3) The bar updates the internal selection, then triggers navigation to search.html with the corresponding value removed from contentTypes or tagSlugs, keeping query and sort, and setting page to 1. 4) The page reloads and searchArticles is called with the reduced filter set, updating the results. 5) If the user clicks a Clear All Filters control, the bar navigates to search.html with only the query (and optionally sort) parameter and no contentTypes or tagSlugs, resetting filters.
interactions: Submitting the header search form navigates to search.html with a query parameter derived from the input value; this triggers new calls to getSearchFilterOptions and searchArticles on page load.

Selecting or deselecting content type checkboxes in the sidebar updates internal selection state; clicking an Apply Filters control uses these selections to build a URL with contentTypes set to the selected values and triggers navigation to search.html.
Selecting or deselecting topic tag checkboxes in the sidebar updates internal tag selection state; clicking Apply Filters navigates to search.html with tagSlugs set to the selected tag slugs.
Changing the sort dropdown triggers navigation to search.html with sort set to the chosen sort value, preserving query and filters, and resetting page to 1.

Clicking on an article title in the results list uses a standard anchor element that navigates to article.html with articleId set to the article's id; standard browser behavior allows Ctrl or Cmd clicks to open in a new tab.
Clicking on a category label within a result card navigates to category.html with categoryId set to the card's categoryId value.

Clicking on a tag chip within a result card triggers navigation to search.html with tagSlugs updated to include or replace with the clicked tag's slug while preserving other parameters as defined by the design.

Clicking the bookmark icon on a card calls toggleBookmarkDefaultReadingList with that article's articleId, then updates the card's isBookmarkedInReadingList-bound UI state and optionally shows a toast using the response message.
Clicking pagination controls builds a URL with an updated page parameter and navigates to search.html to load the selected page of results using searchArticles.
Clicking on active filter chips' remove controls modifies the filter-related URL parameters and navigates, effectively removing specific content type or tag filters.
Clicking a Clear All Filters control removes contentTypes and tagSlugs from the URL and navigates, leaving only the query (and optionally sort) to refresh the results without filters.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load and on every navigation to search.html, the page controller parses the URL query parameters query, contentTypes, tagSlugs, sort, and page. If query is present and non-empty, the controller calls getSearchFilterOptions once to load contentTypeOptions, topicTags, and availableSortOptions. It then determines the current filter state: selected content types come from parsing the contentTypes string into a list (based on comma separators), and selected topic tags come from parsing tagSlugs similarly. The current sort mode is taken directly from the sort parameter if provided, or a default derived from getSearchFilterOptions or internal configuration if not. The current page is taken from the page parameter if provided and valid, otherwise page defaults to 1. Using this derived state, the controller calls searchArticles with the following arguments: query (from URL), contentTypes (as a comma-separated string or null if no content types are selected), tagSlugs (as a comma-separated string or null if none), sort (current sort value), page (numeric page), and pageSize (an implementation-defined constant or configuration value not hardcoded in the design). The searchArticles response determines the rendered results list, totalCount, page, and pageSize, which are bound to the results list and pagination components. Whenever the user interacts with filters, sort, or pagination, no state is stored in localStorage; instead, the components compute updated URL parameter values and trigger a full navigation to search.html with the new parameters. This ensures that the URL is always the single source of truth for the current search state, enabling bookmarking and sharing of a specific search view. Bookmark state per article is obtained from the isBookmarkedInReadingList field returned by searchArticles; when toggleBookmarkDefaultReadingList is called after a user action, the card's local bookmark UI state is updated immediately based on the response without changing URL parameters, since bookmark membership does not affect the result set. Reading status is read-only on this page and is taken directly from the readingStatus field in each searchArticles result.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL parsing, API calls, and state distribution to child components on search.html. On load or when navigated to, it reads URL parameters for query, contentTypes, tagSlugs, sort, and page, calls getSearchFilterOptions to retrieve available filter and sort options, then calls searchArticles with the derived state to fetch the current page of results. It passes data and callback handlers to all other components and re-invokes searchArticles when navigation returns to this page with updated URL parameters.
data_binding: URLParameters: query, contentTypes, tagSlugs, sort, page
searchArticles.results (Article-like objects with category and tag info)

components.functionality: Provides the global search input in the header for entering or editing the search query while on search.html. It initializes its input value from the query URL parameter and, on submission, navigates to search.html with the new query value and resets or omits other parameters according to default behavior, causing the page controller to run a new search.

Displays a summary of the current search context including the query text, the number of results returned, the current sort label, and a Clear All Filters control. It uses searchArticles.totalCount and the current URL parameters to show dynamic information and allows clearing of contentTypes and tagSlugs filters in one action.

Renders a list of content type filter options as selectable controls (for example, checkboxes) in the sidebar. Options and labels are sourced from getSearchFilterOptions.contentTypeOptions. The current selection is determined by the contentTypes URL parameter. Users can adjust selections and then apply them, which updates the contentTypes parameter in the URL and triggers a navigation to reload results.

Displays available topic tags as a filter list in the sidebar using the Tag data provided by getSearchFilterOptions.topicTags. It maps the tagSlugs URL parameter to a selected state for the corresponding tags. Users can select or deselect multiple tags, and applying changes updates the tagSlugs parameter in the URL to reflect the set of selected tag slugs and navigates to reload results.

Shows currently active content type and topic tag filters as removable chips. It derives filter values from contentTypes and tagSlugs URL parameters and uses getSearchFilterOptions contentTypeOptions and topicTags to resolve human-readable labels. Removing an individual chip updates the corresponding URL parameter and navigates to search.html with the adjusted filter set; a Clear All action delegates to the summary bar or page controller to clear all filters.

Provides a dropdown or similar control for choosing the sort mode of search results. It populates its options using getSearchFilterOptions.availableSortOptions and initializes the selected value from the sort URL parameter or a default. When changed, it triggers navigation to search.html with the updated sort value and resets page to 1.

Renders the collection of article search results as a list of cards. It iterates over searchArticles.results and instantiates a search-result-card component for each item, providing the data and handlers necessary for navigation, bookmarking, and tag interactions.

Represents a single article in the search results list. It displays the article title (linking to article.html with articleId), category label (linking to category.html with categoryId), summary, tags, reading time, rating, comment count, difficulty, reading status, and bookmark state. It allows users to navigate to the article or category, filter by clicking tags, and toggle bookmark state via toggleBookmarkDefaultReadingList.

Displays pagination controls for the search results list. It uses totalCount, page, and pageSize from searchArticles to calculate the total number of pages and highlights the current page. When the user selects another page, it navigates to search.html with the same query, filters, and sort but updates the page parameter to the selected value.

Encapsulates the logic for toggling bookmark state for articles shown in the search results. When triggered by a search-result-card, it calls toggleBookmarkDefaultReadingList with the articleId, updates the associated card's isBookmarkedInReadingList state based on the response, and informs the bookmark-status-toast component to display feedback.

Shows transient, non-blocking feedback messages after bookmark actions, such as confirming that an article was added to or removed from the default Reading List. It listens for events from the bookmark-toggle-handler and displays the textual message provided by toggleBookmarkDefaultReadingList, then hides after a short duration.

At the top of the right column, the results summary bar, active filters bar, and sort control form a compact control band, followed by a vertical list of search result cards and, at the bottom, pagination controls.

At the top of the right column, the results summary bar, active filters bar, and sort control form a compact control band, followed by a vertical list of search result cards and, at the bottom, pagination controls.

From there the user’s attention drops into the main container, first hitting the results summary and the active filters/sort controls at the top of the right column. As the user scrolls, the main vertical scan follows the list of result cards, while the left sidebar filters create a secondary, periodic focal path down the page, echoing a subtle F-pattern of headline → controls → list, with optional glances to the filters when refining the search.

layout.overall_description: The search results page sits within a clean, minimalist shell that echoes a modern cybersecurity blog. At the very top, a fixed horizontal navigation spans the full width; below it, a dark, moody hero section fills roughly the top quarter of the viewport. Inside this hero, content is left-aligned within a centered max-width container: a small eyebrow or label such as “Search Results” appears above a prominent heading showing the current query, with the main search form placed directly beneath as a wide but not full-width input, reinforcing the page’s purpose. Dropping below the hero, the main content area is presented inside a centered container with generous lateral gutters. This container is split into two columns: a narrower left sidebar holding stacked filter cards (content type filters above, topic/tag filters below) and a wider right column dedicated to the results. At the top of the right column, a horizontal band of controls combines a concise results summary, a row of active filter chips aligned to the left, and a sort dropdown aligned to the right, all with subtle separators and ample breathing room. Beneath this control band, search result cards are listed vertically with consistent spacing, each card a simple rectangular block with a bold title, concise metadata, a short excerpt, and a bookmark icon sitting unobtrusively near the top-right corner. The entire list feels airy yet structured, with whitespace doing most of the section separation. At the bottom of the main column, centered pagination controls appear with extra vertical margin to clearly signal the end of a result set. A small, unobtrusive toast notification can briefly appear near the lower edge of the viewport when bookmarking actions occur, floating above the content without shifting layout, preserving the clean, editorial feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller establishes the overall frame: a full-width, fixed-position top navigation (defined elsewhere) sits at the very top, with the hero section immediately beneath it, also full-width. Below the hero, it centers the main content within a max-width container (around 1100–1200px) with generous side gutters so that content never touches the viewport edges. Inside this container, it defines a responsive two-column grid where the left column (filters) occupies roughly 25–30% of the container width and the right column (results) occupies the remaining 70–75%. On larger screens, both columns appear side by side with aligned top edges; on smaller screens or narrow viewports, the layout gracefully collapses to a single-column stack where the filters appear above the results, preserving the linear reading flow. Vertical spacing between structural sections (hero → main container, top controls → results list, results list → pagination) follows the 32–48px rhythm, ensuring that each section is clearly delineated by whitespace rather than heavy borders.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Manages the page’s structural grid and responsive breakpoints. Ensures the navigation stays fixed at the top while the rest of the content scrolls beneath it. Controls max-width centering of the main content and column stacking behavior on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header search form is positioned within the hero section, left-aligned inside the same centered content column used elsewhere. It sits directly beneath the hero’s main heading (such as “Results for ‘X’”), with about 16–24px vertical space separating the heading from the form. The form itself spans roughly 60–70% of the content width on desktop, creating a substantial but not wall-to-wall input field that visually anchors the hero without feeling heavy. It is horizontally aligned so that its left edge matches the left edge of the hero title and the main content column below, reinforcing a strong vertical alignment line down the page. Above and below the form, there is comfortable whitespace (24–32px below before the hero’s bottom edge), keeping it visually prominent yet not cramped, and clearly signaling that the search can be refined at any time.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero area; does not scroll independently. On smaller screens, it expands toward nearly full width while maintaining padding from viewport edges and remains stacked beneath the hero heading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary bar is placed at the top of the main content area, visually bridging the hero and the two-column region. Within the centered container, it appears above the two-column split but aligned to the same left edge as the future content columns, occupying the full usable width of that container. It typically displays the query summary and total result count, arranged horizontally on a single line where possible, with around 16–24px of padding above and below the text. There is approximately 24–32px of vertical space between the bottom of the hero and this bar, and another 16–24px between the summary bar and the two-column content area beneath, making it feel like a distinct yet lightweight informational band. The treatment is subtle—perhaps a faint bottom border or a slight tonal shift in background—so it provides structure without competing visually with the hero or the results themselves.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal band at the top of the content; scrolls away with the rest of the page content. On smaller screens it becomes a single-line or two-line text block but keeps full-width presence within the content container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content-type filter sidebar occupies the top section of the left column within the main content container. It is implemented as a clean, rectangular card aligned to the left column’s boundaries, with 16–24px internal padding on all sides and a simple heading such as “Content Type” at the top. Its width is defined by the left column (roughly 25–30% of the main container), and its height adjusts to its content, usually shorter than the viewport. There is a 24–32px gap between the results summary bar above and the top of this filter card, ensuring a clear break from the summary band. Below this card, a similar vertical gap (24–32px) separates it from the topic-tag filter sidebar, visually grouping filter blocks without crowding. The card uses either a light background or a very subtle border to differentiate itself from the page background while remaining visually lightweight.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column but can optionally be made sticky on tall viewports, staying visible as the user scrolls the results column. On narrow viewports where columns stack, it appears above the results controls and list, maintaining the top-of-flow filter position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic-tag filter sidebar is placed directly beneath the content-type filter card in the left column, aligned to the same left and right edges, forming a vertical stack of filter modules. It mirrors the same card-like treatment with 16–24px padding and a clear heading, such as “Topics” or “Tags,” followed by a grid or vertical list of tag chips. A vertical gap of approximately 24–32px separates it from the content-type filter above, maintaining the rhythm of distinct but related sections. Its width matches the left column, and its height may be taller if many tags are shown; in that case, its internal content can scroll or wrap while the card body remains visually unified. The visual style remains light—whitespace first, with a possible subtle bottom border or tonal background to demarcate the card from the main page background without introducing heavy visual weight.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inside the left column, optionally participating in a sticky sidebar behavior if the entire filter stack is made sticky. On smaller screens when columns collapse, it appears immediately below the content-type filter card and above the results controls, forming a clean linear filter-first flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active-filters bar sits at the top of the right (main) column, just below the results summary bar and horizontally aligned with the top of the left sidebar filters. It spans the full width of the right column, with content (filter chips) left-aligned and some breathing room: about 12–16px padding on the top and bottom and 16–24px from the left and right column edges. Active filters appear as a row (or multiple wrapped rows) of compact chips with a small gap between them, and there is roughly 8–12px of separation between this bar and the sort control when they share a horizontal row, or 16px if stacked vertically on smaller screens. The bar is visually understated—usually a simple text-and-chip row with possibly a faint dividing line beneath—to keep focus on the results while still making the current filter state easy to scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the right column, it can be grouped with the sort control into a control band that may become sticky at the top of the viewport once scrolled into place, ensuring that active filters remain visible as the user scrolls the results list. On small screens, chips wrap and the bar increases in height as needed without overlapping other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is positioned in the same horizontal band as the active-filters bar at the top of the right column. On wider screens, it is right-aligned within that band, on the same baseline as the first row of filter chips, creating a balanced left-right tension: active filters to the left, sort dropdown to the right. There is a modest horizontal gap (~8–16px) between the chips and the sort control, and the whole band has 12–16px of vertical padding for a light but consistent presence. The sort dropdown itself is compact, using minimal chrome so it does not visually dominate the band. On smaller screens where horizontal space is constrained, the sort control stacks below the active filters, left-aligned with about 8–12px vertical spacing between them, maintaining a clear vertical reading order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in the same sticky control band behavior as the active-filters bar when enabled, remaining visible at the top of the viewport during scroll. Its width is intrinsic to its label and options, staying relatively narrow so it doesn’t disturb the layout of the results column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search results list occupies the main vertical body of the right column below the control band (active filters and sort control). It spans the full available width of the right column and begins about 24–32px beneath the bottom of the control band to clearly delineate the transition from controls to content.
