Inside this region, result cards are laid out in a single-column stack, one per row, each extending from the left to the right edge of the right column minus standard internal padding. Vertical spacing between cards is consistent, typically 24–32px, allowing each card to feel distinct while preserving a smooth scroll rhythm suited for reading. The list stretches downward as many cards as are available for the current page, leaving enough whitespace at the bottom before the pagination controls (32–40px) to visually signal the end of the current set.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls as a continuous vertical list within the main page scroll; no independent scrollbars. On smaller screens, it remains a single column but expands closer to the full viewport width while maintaining side padding specified by the main container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each search result card is a rectangular content block within the results list, aligned flush with the right column’s left and right edges and surrounded by comfortable whitespace. The card uses 16–24px of internal padding on all sides, with a clear typographic hierarchy: the title appears at the top left, set in a bold style and allowed to span multiple lines if needed; below it, with about 8–12px vertical spacing, a line of metadata (such as date, reading time, or content type) is displayed in a subdued style; beneath that, another 8–12px down, a short excerpt of 2–3 lines provides context. The optional bookmark icon is placed near the top-right corner within the card’s padding box, visually aligned with the title’s first line, creating a subtle action hotspot without overpowering the content. Cards are separated vertically by approximately 24–32px, with no heavy borders; at most, they may have a very light outline or a soft background tint to distinguish them from the page background, in keeping with the minimalist aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static blocks within the vertical list; each card is fully clickable or primarily title-clickable without shifting layout on interaction. The bookmark icon’s state changes visually (e.g., filled vs outlined) without resizing the icon or the card, ensuring no layout shift when toggled.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are positioned at the bottom of the right column, directly beneath the last search result card and separated from it by a generous vertical gap of about 32–40px. They are horizontally centered within the right column so that page numbers and navigation arrows form a compact, symmetric cluster. The controls themselves are minimal: small, evenly spaced page links or buttons with 8–12px internal padding, and modest gaps (8–12px) between them, avoiding dominant visual weight. There is additional whitespace (24–32px) below the pagination group before reaching the bottom of the page content, giving the end of the results a calm, finished feeling rather than an abrupt stop.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the results column; appears after the last card and scrolls normally with the page. On narrower screens, the pagination controls may wrap to multiple lines while staying center-aligned within the content width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmark-toggle handler has no visible UI of its own; instead, it binds behavior to the bookmark icons within each search result card. Conceptually, its interaction target is the small icon situated at the top-right corner of each card’s content area, comfortably inside the card’s padding so that it is easy to tap or click without interfering with the text. The handler ensures that toggling the bookmark state updates only the icon’s visual style (such as color or fill) and triggers a non-intrusive toast, without causing the card or surrounding layout to reflow. This keeps the visual structure of the results column stable while providing quick feedback on bookmarking actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely behavioral; it does not occupy its own spatial region in the layout. Ensures interactions on the bookmark icons are handled without introducing overlays or additional components that would alter the page’s spatial arrangement.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmark status toast appears as a small, unobtrusive notification overlay when a bookmark is added or removed. It is positioned near the lower-right quadrant of the viewport (or lower center on very small screens), floating above the main content without pushing any elements aside. The toast is a horizontal pill or slim rectangle with 12–16px of internal padding and a concise message line, no wider than necessary (typically 260–320px), and uses a slightly darker, semi-opaque background to stand out against the page while respecting the minimalist style. It sits with a margin of approximately 24–32px from the viewport’s bottom and side edges, mirroring the page’s overall spacing rhythm. Only one toast is visible at a time, and it fades in and out smoothly, so it does not disrupt the clean reading experience of the results list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlay element positioned fixed relative to the viewport, independent of page scroll. It appears briefly on bookmark actions and then auto-dismisses, without affecting the layout or scroll position of underlying content.

name: Article Detail
title: Article Detail
description: Displays a single article with full content, metadata, reading tools (status, bookmarking, collections), comments, sharing, and feedback for a personal cybersecurity / hacking blog.

core_features: Load and display full article content and metadata (title, category, tags, content type, published/updated dates, reading time, upvotes, difficulty, comment count, rating) using getArticleDetails.
Show article category and tags with navigation to the category listing and other article lists using URL parameters.
Provide a bookmark toggle that adds/removes the article from the default Reading List using toggleBookmarkDefaultReadingList.

Provide an 'Add to collection' dialog that lists existing collections and supports adding the article to a selected collection via addArticleToCollection or creating a new collection and adding the article using createCollectionAndAddArticle.
Allow changing the article reading status via a dropdown (not_started, in_progress, completed) using setArticleReadingStatus, with the current value from getArticleDetails.
Show a share toolbar near the article title with a Copy link action that uses copyArticleLinkToClipboard.
Display comments with pagination using getArticleComments and allow posting new comments using postArticleComment with minimum length validation.

Provide a feedback section with a star rating selector, multiple-choice feedback reasons from getFeedbackReasons, and an optional text comment, submitted via submitArticleFeedback and reflected in updated average rating and rating count.
For review-type articles, display the recommendedTools array as a clear list so users can easily see and count each distinct recommended tool name.
For CTF write-up articles, display challenge metadata (year, difficulty, upvotes) in a dedicated metadata block and surface detailed practice steps from the article content.

For series-part articles, display series metadata (series title, part number, total parts) with a link to the series overview (series_detail.html) and highlight sections like prerequisites within the article content.
user_workflows: View an article and its metadata: 1) User lands on article.html with articleId (and optional slug) in the URL. 2) Page calls getArticleDetails with articleId. 3) Article header and content are rendered, including category, tags, reading time, upvotes, difficulty, series info, and recommended tools/CTF metadata when applicable.

Toggle bookmark in default Reading List: 1) User clicks the bookmark icon in the article header. 2) Page calls toggleBookmarkDefaultReadingList with articleId. 3) UI updates the bookmark icon state based on isBookmarked in the response and may show a confirmation state. 4) User can navigate to reading_list.html via header/profile menu if desired.

Add article to a specific collection: 1) User clicks the 'Add to collection' button. 2) Dialog opens and calls getCollectionsOverview (if collections not already loaded in this session). 3) User selects an existing collection and confirms. 4) Page calls addArticleToCollection with articleId and collectionId. 5) UI shows success or 'already in collection' messages and offers a link to reading_list.html with collectionId as a URL parameter when appropriate.

Create a new collection and add the article: 1) In the 'Add to collection' dialog, user chooses the create-new option and enters a collection name and optional description. 2) User submits. 3) Page calls createCollectionAndAddArticle with articleId, name, and description. 4) On success, dialog is updated with the newly created collection and indicates that the article is now in it, optionally providing a 'View collection' navigation to reading_list.html with collectionId.

Update reading status: 1) User opens the status dropdown in the article header. 2) User selects a different status value. 3) Page calls setArticleReadingStatus with articleId and the selected readingStatus. 4) On success, the displayed status is updated; on failure, UI reverts to the previous value and shows an error state.

Copy shareable link: 1) User opens the share toolbar near the title. 2) User clicks the 'Copy link' action. 3) Page calls copyArticleLinkToClipboard with articleId. 4) On success, UI shows a temporary confirmation message indicating the URL is copied.

Read and post comments: 1) On initial load, page calls getArticleComments with articleId, default page, and pageSize. 2) Comments section displays totalCount and the first page of comments. 3) User navigates between comment pages; each navigation calls getArticleComments with updated page. 4) To post, user types a comment of at least the minimum length into the comment form and submits. 5) Page calls postArticleComment with articleId and content. 6) On success, the new comment is inserted according to the chosen placement (e.g., at the top of the current page), and the comment count is updated using newTotalCommentCount.

Submit article feedback: 1) When the feedback section becomes visible, page calls getFeedbackReasons once to populate the reasons list. 2) User selects a star rating (including the ability to select exactly 4 stars), chooses one or more reasons, and optionally enters a text comment of sufficient length. 3) User submits the feedback. 4) Page calls submitArticleFeedback with articleId, ratingStars, reasonCodes, and comment. 5) On success, UI updates the displayed averageRating and ratingCount using the response and prevents duplicate submissions based on canSubmitFeedback.

Use series navigation for series parts: 1) For articles where series data is present, the header shows series title and part numbering. 2) User clicks the series title link. 3) Browser navigates to series_detail.html with seriesId as a URL parameter, using values from getArticleDetails.series.

Use CTF metadata for practice: 1) For write-up articles, a CTF metadata block displays challengeYear, difficulty, and upvotes from getArticleDetails. 2) User uses this metadata and the structured practice steps from contentHtml to decide how and when to practice the challenge.

Use category/tags navigation: 1) User clicks the category label in the article header. 2) Browser navigates to category.html with categoryId set from getArticleDetails. 3) User can later return to article.html via links in category or other listing pages using URL parameters.

Search from article view: 1) User types a term into the global search bar on this page. 2) Submitting the search triggers navigation to search.html with query in the URL parameters; article.html does not perform search itself.

Navigate to Reading List or a specific collection after saving: 1) After bookmarking or adding to a collection, user clicks a contextual link or button provided in the UI. 2) Browser navigates to reading_list.html, optionally including collectionId to focus on a specific collection.
interactions: Page load: On page initialization, read articleId (and optionally slug) from the URL query string. If articleId is present, call getArticleDetails with it. Use the returned data to populate article header, content, metadata, reading status, and bookmark state.
Category click: When user clicks the category label, trigger navigation to category.html, setting categoryId in the query string using the categoryId from getArticleDetails.
Series breadcrumb click: When user clicks the series title or breadcrumb on a series-part article, navigate to series_detail.html with seriesId from getArticleDetails.series.seriesId.

Bookmark icon click: When user clicks the bookmark icon, call toggleBookmarkDefaultReadingList with articleId. Use the isBookmarked flag to update the icon’s active/inactive state. Optionally, show the returned collectionName and totalItemsInCollection in a short inline message.

Open 'Add to collection' dialog: When user clicks the 'Add to collection' button, open a dialog and, if collections are not already loaded in this page session, call getCollectionsOverview. Populate the dialog list with the returned collections and visually indicate which collections already contain the article using collectionsContaining from getArticleDetails.

Select collection in dialog: When user selects an existing collection and confirms, call addArticleToCollection with articleId and the selected collectionId. If success and wasAlreadyInCollection is false, mark that collection as containing the article; if true, show a non-blocking message indicating the article was already in that collection. Offer an optional 'View collection' action that navigates to reading_list.html with collectionId.

Create new collection in dialog: When user chooses to create a new collection, validate that the name field is non-empty (and meets any length constraints). On submit, call createCollectionAndAddArticle with articleId, name, and description. If success and articleAdded is true, add the returned collection to the dialog list, mark it as containing the article, and optionally focus it. Provide an optional 'View collection' navigation to reading_list.html with the returned collectionId.

Reading status change: When the reading status dropdown value is changed, immediately call setArticleReadingStatus with articleId and the newly selected readingStatus. Show a transient loading state while the update is pending. If success is true, reflect the new status; if not, revert the dropdown to the previous value and show an error indicator.

Share toolbar copy link: When user clicks the 'Copy link' action in the share toolbar, call copyArticleLinkToClipboard with articleId. Based on success, show a short confirmation or error message. No navigation occurs.

Comments pagination: When the comments section is first rendered, call getArticleComments with articleId, default page (e.g., 1), and pageSize. When user navigates to another page via pagination controls, call getArticleComments with the updated page parameter. Update the displayed comments and keep totalCount consistent with the response.

Post new comment: When user submits the comment form, validate that the comment text length meets the minimum (based on requirements tied to ArticleComment). If valid and canComment from getArticleDetails is true, call postArticleComment with articleId and content. On success, insert the new comment into the comments list, update the comment counter using newTotalCommentCount, clear the input field, and optionally scroll to the new comment.
Feedback reasons load: On first expansion or visibility of the feedback section, call getFeedbackReasons to populate multiple-choice options. Cache them in page state for subsequent opens.

Feedback input interactions: When user selects a star rating, update a local ratingStars state. Clicking on a specific star sets ratingStars to that star’s value (including 4). Clicking the same star again can optionally re-confirm or allow deselection depending on chosen behavior (defined in component logic). Selecting/deselecting feedback reasons updates a local reasonCodes list. Text input updates a local comment value with optional length validation.

Submit feedback: When the feedback form is submitted, validate that a ratingStars value is present and, if a comment is supplied, that it meets any required minimum length associated with ArticleFeedback. If valid and canSubmitFeedback is true, call submitArticleFeedback with articleId, ratingStars, reasonCodes, and comment. On success, update displayed averageRating and ratingCount with newAverageRating and newRatingCount, and optionally lock the form or show a 'feedback submitted' state.

Recommended tools interaction for reviews: For articles where contentType indicates reviews and recommendedTools is non-empty, display recommendedTools as a structured list, each item bound to a single entry from the array so users can reliably see and count each recommended tool. No additional API calls are required for user interaction beyond normal scrolling.

CTF metadata interaction for write-ups: For articles where contentType indicates write_ups and challengeYear or difficulty is present, display a CTF metadata block bound to these fields and upvotes from getArticleDetails. The user can use this metadata while reading the practice steps within contentHtml without additional API calls.

Series part interaction: For articles with series data, display series title, part number, and totalParts from getArticleDetails.series. Users can click the series title to navigate; no additional API calls from this page are required after navigation.

Global search submit: When the header/global search form is submitted, read the entered term and navigate to search.html with query set to that term in the URL. The article page does not call any search interface directly.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary page identity is determined by URL query parameters. On every load, the page parses the query string to obtain articleId (required for functionality) and slug (optional). If articleId is missing, no data-loading interfaces are called and the page can show a generic 'article not available' state managed locally.

Once articleId is available, getArticleDetails is called exactly with this articleId; the slug is not sent to any interface but can be used only for client-side behaviors such as updating the browser URL to a canonical format using the returned slug, without affecting data loading.

All article-level state (title, contentHtml, categoryId, categoryName, contentType, tags, series, challengeYear, recommendedTools, readingStatus, isBookmarkedInReadingList, collectionsContaining, canComment, canSubmitFeedback) is derived from the single getArticleDetails response and lives in in-memory page state for the duration of the session on this page. When users navigate away (e.g., to category.html, series_detail.html, reading_list.html, search.html), the new page will have its own URL parameters and state.

Comment list state is also URL and parameter driven: the current comments page number is held in page state and used as the page parameter in getArticleComments. Changing the comments page updates this local page value and triggers a new call. The articleId from the URL remains the key for comments. totalCount and comment pages are not persisted beyond this page view.

Reading status, bookmark status, and collection membership are not persisted locally across reloads beyond what is reflected in the server responses. After any mutation (setArticleReadingStatus, toggleBookmarkDefaultReadingList, addArticleToCollection, createCollectionAndAddArticle, submitArticleFeedback, postArticleComment), the page updates only its in-memory UI state using returned fields; a full page reload would re-derive the latest stable state from getArticleDetails and subsequent API calls if needed. No localStorage or other persistent client-side storage is used for this page; all persistent state is server-side and accessed via the assigned interfaces.

Navigation to other pages uses URL parameters only: category.html receives categoryId based on articleCategoryId from getArticleDetails; series_detail.html receives seriesId from getArticleDetails.series; reading_list.html optionally receives collectionId from actions within the collection dialog; search.html receives query from the global search form. These navigations do not modify state on article.html beyond causing an unload.

components.functionality: Reads articleId (and optional slug) from the URL query string on initialization, calls getArticleDetails with articleId, and stores the returned article details in page state for use by all other components.

Displays the article title, category label, tags, primary metadata (publishedAt, updatedAt, readingTimeMinutes, upvotes, difficulty, commentCount, averageRating, ratingCount), and content-type-specific flags. Provides navigation via the category label and exposes series information when present.

Shows the current readingStatus from the article and allows the user to change it via a dropdown control, updating the server state through setArticleReadingStatus and reflecting the new status on success.

Shows whether the article is bookmarked in the default Reading List using isBookmarkedInReadingList from getArticleDetails and allows the user to toggle that state with a single click using toggleBookmarkDefaultReadingList.

When opened, displays all available collections using getCollectionsOverview and the subset that already contains this article using collectionsContaining. Allows the user to add the article to an existing collection via addArticleToCollection or create a new collection and add the article via createCollectionAndAddArticle. Offers navigation to reading_list.html with optional collectionId for the selected collection.

Provides sharing actions for the article, including a Copy link control that uses copyArticleLinkToClipboard to copy the canonical URL for articleId to the clipboard and shows a confirmation message based on the result.

Renders the article summary and contentHtml. Based on contentType, it conditionally includes sections for review tools, CTF metadata, and series context. It does not call APIs itself but reacts to article data provided by article-loader.
For review-type articles, reads the recommendedTools array from the article data and renders each tool as a distinct list item so users can easily see and count the distinct recommended tools.

For write-up articles, displays CTF-specific metadata such as challengeYear, difficulty, and upvotes using the Article fields, helping users assess challenge context before or while following the practice steps in the article content.

For articles that belong to a series, shows the series title, seriesPartNumber, and totalParts. Provides a clickable series breadcrumb that navigates to series_detail.html with seriesId via URL parameters.

Displays a paginated list of comments for the current article using getArticleComments, shows totalCount, and manages pagination state. Integrates with the comment form to insert new comments on successful submission.

Allows the user to enter and submit a new comment for the article. Validates that the comment meets the minimum length requirement before calling postArticleComment and, on success, updates the comments-section component and the article comment count.

Provides star rating selection, multiple-choice feedback reasons (from getFeedbackReasons), and an optional text comment field. Handles validation and submission of feedback to submitArticleFeedback and updates displayed average rating and rating count using the response.
Header-level form that captures a search term and navigates the browser to search.html with query set as a URL parameter; it does not make any API calls itself on this page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid similar to Bootstrap: a full-width container for the hero and navigation, then a centered content container with a ~3/9 or 4/8 column split between sidebar and main content.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the linear flow of the mockup: a fixed horizontal navigation at the very top containing the global search on the right, then a full-width dark hero for the article header, followed by a centered main content container that splits into a narrower left sidebar and a wider right article column. The hero houses the article title and primary metadata plus reading tools (status, bookmark, share). Below, the main container holds, on the left, contextual panels (CTF metadata and recommended tools) and, on the right, the article body, series info, comments, and feedback stacked vertically.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the linear flow of the mockup: a fixed horizontal navigation at the very top containing the global search on the right, then a full-width dark hero for the article header, followed by a centered main content container that splits into a narrower left sidebar and a wider right article column. The hero houses the article title and primary metadata plus reading tools (status, bookmark, share). Below, the main container holds, on the left, contextual panels (CTF metadata and recommended tools) and, on the right, the article body, series info, comments, and feedback stacked vertically.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The nav and hero span the full width. The hero occupies roughly the top quarter of the initial viewport height, with its content centered in a max-width container (~1100–1200px) and generous left/right gutters (~10–15% of the viewport each side). Below the hero, the main container is centered and uses a 3/9 to 4/8 grid: the sidebar takes ~25–30% width and the main article column ~70–75%. Vertical gaps between major sections (hero → main content, article → comments, comments → feedback) are about 32–48px; inner spacing within cards/panels uses 16–24px padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density is editorial and moderately spacious: comfortable line height for reading, clear vertical rhythm, and separation by whitespace rather than heavy borders. Each module (metadata panel, tools list, comments block) sits in a visually distinct rectangular block with subtle dividers (thin lines or background shifts) and 16–24px internal padding. Controls like reading status and bookmark are compact but not cramped, spaced by 8–16px.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s Z-pattern: the eye starts at the logo/navigation on the top left, moves across to the global search on the right, then drops diagonally to the large left-aligned article title in the hero. From there the user moves slightly right to the inline reading tools and share controls, then continues down into the main content. In the main area, an F-pattern emerges in the right column as the user scans from the series breadcrumb at the top of the article, down through headings and paragraphs. Periodic glances to the left sidebar (CTF metadata and tools) create a secondary rhythm similar to the original sidebar+content relationship.

layout.overall_description: The article detail page presents as a clean, modern cybersecurity blog layout. A fixed, full-width navigation bar sits at the very top, with the site identity on the left and a lean global search field tucked to the right. Directly beneath, a full-width, dark-toned hero spans the screen, hinting at a moody, hacker aesthetic. Within this hero, a centered content band holds a large, left-aligned article title with concise metadata just below it, while reading status, bookmarking, and share controls align to the right, forming a subtle Z-shaped focal line. Below the hero, the page tightens into a centered max-width container that splits into two columns: a narrower left sidebar with compact panels for CTF metadata and recommended tools, and a wider right column that flows through series information, the article body, and then into comments and feedback. Generous vertical spacing and soft separators keep the layout airy and readable, while the constant left alignment of key text creates an easy top-to-bottom reading path. Loading and collection dialogs appear as light overlays on top of this structure without disturbing the underlying grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article loader appears as a full-bleed overlay that covers the content area from just below the fixed top navigation down to the bottom edge of the viewport (and extends further if the page scrolls). It spans 100% of the page width and height, dimming the hero and main content with a semi-transparent dark layer. At the center of the viewport, a minimalist spinner or loading indicator is positioned, surrounded by ample whitespace (at least 64px clear in all directions), ensuring it remains the focal point. The overlay is visually flat, with no heavy shadows, and disappears entirely once the article data is loaded, revealing the hero and two-column layout beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay that sits above all article content (including hero and main container), aligned to the viewport rather than the document; it blocks interaction while active and scrolls independently of the underlying content (content behind remains static and non-interactive).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article header and metadata sit within the full-width hero section, centered horizontally inside a max-width content band. The hero’s background is dark and subtly textured or gradient, stretching edge-to-edge across the viewport. Inside, starting roughly 25–30% down from the top of the hero, the article title is left-aligned, occupying about 60–70% of the content width, with large, bold typography. Below the title, a compact metadata row (author name, publication date, reading time, tags as needed) runs horizontally, also left-aligned and spanning 50–60% of the width. The metadata row has comfortable vertical breathing room: 16–24px below the title and 16–24px above the area where the reading controls sit on the right. The header content maintains generous left and right padding (24–32px) relative to the container edges, matching the mockup’s editorial spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section, aligned to the central content container; it scrolls away with the page while the navigation bar remains fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading status control is placed within the hero, horizontally aligned with the metadata row but anchored toward the right side of the same central content container. It appears as a compact, pill-like dropdown or button group about 120–160px wide, sitting on a single line or on a second row just below the metadata depending on screen width. On large screens, it aligns to the right edge of the article header area, approximately in line with the bottom edge of the article title or metadata, creating a visual balance across the hero: title and meta on the left, reading tools on the right. A horizontal gap of 8–16px separates it from the bookmark toggle, and about 16–24px separates this cluster vertically from the bottom edge of the hero, keeping it visually anchored but not cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element inside the hero’s right side; on narrower screens it drops beneath the metadata and aligns left under the title, preserving the top-to-bottom reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmark toggle sits directly adjacent to the reading status control within the same hero header tools cluster on the right. It appears as a small icon button or compact labeled control, roughly the size of a single line of text (32–40px in height), positioned to the immediate right of the reading status control with an 8–12px horizontal gap. Together they form a tight horizontal group that visually reads as a single control area. This group aligns with the right edge of the content container, leaving 24–32px of padding to the hero’s right boundary. Vertically, the bookmark toggle shares the same baseline as the reading status control, and the entire group sits slightly below or aligned with the metadata row, maintaining an orderly rectangular block of header elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element within the hero’s tool cluster; on smaller screens it wraps under the reading status control, aligning left to maintain a clean vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collection dialog appears centered over the entire page, floating above the hero and main content. It is a rectangular modal panel occupying approximately 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop (max-width around 480–560px) and about 60–70% of the viewport height if content is long, with internal padding of 24–32px on all sides. It is horizontally and vertically centered, framed by a subtle, darkened backdrop that covers the whole viewport. Inside the dialog, the title (e.g., “Save to collection”) is aligned to the top-left, with form fields and collection lists stacked vertically below, separated by 8–16px spacing. A row of actions (Save/Cancel) anchors the bottom, right-aligned. The modal’s edge has a minimal border or subtle background contrast, consistent with the flat, minimalist style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position modal overlay that sits above all other elements, including the navigation bar; the background is dimmed and non-interactive while the dialog is open, and the modal scrolls internally if its content exceeds its height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The share toolbar is placed at the lower-right portion of the hero content, directly beneath the bookmarking and reading status controls or on the same vertical band depending on space. It forms a horizontal row of 3–5 minimalist icon buttons (for social platforms and copy link), each 32–40px square, with 8–12px gaps between them. The toolbar aligns to the right of the central content container, matching the right alignment of the reading tools, and sits about 8–16px below the reading-status/bookmark cluster if placed on a separate row. There is a comfortable 16–24px gap between the share toolbar and the bottom edge of the hero, ensuring it feels part of the header area but not pressed against the section boundary. On narrow screens, the share toolbar wraps onto its own line under the metadata and aligns left, forming a compact horizontal strip above the start of the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element within the hero; behaves responsively by wrapping to its own row and left-aligning on smaller viewports to preserve the vertical reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article body occupies the primary right-hand column of the main content container, directly beneath the hero. This column starts about 32–48px below the bottom of the hero, maintaining a clear separation. It spans roughly 70–75% of the container width, leaving the remaining 25–30% for the left sidebar. Within this column, the article text is left-aligned with a comfortable line length (approximately 60–80 characters per line) and line height tuned for reading. Headings, paragraphs, code blocks, and images are stacked vertically with 12–24px spacing between elements, grouped into logical sections. The article body has lateral padding of 16–24px from the main column edge, creating a subtle inner text column and ensuring that content doesn’t run edge-to-edge. At the very top of this area, there is a small gap (16–24px) below the series-part-info if present; at the bottom, there is 32–40px of whitespace before the comments section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block occupying the full height of the right main column; scrolls naturally with the page and defines the vertical rhythm for content below it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recommended tools list is presented as a distinct sidebar block in the left column of the main content container. It sits directly below the CTF metadata block, separated from it by a 24–32px vertical gap. The block occupies the full width of the sidebar (approximately 25–30% of the main container) and has a subtle card-like appearance: a slightly lighter or darker background than the page, with 16–24px padding on all sides and perhaps a thin top border or header label (“Tools used in this article”). Inside the block, each tool is listed in a vertical stack with 8–12px spacing between items, possibly accompanied by small inline descriptions. The overall block height adjusts to its content but is generally shorter than the article column, ending above or around mid-height of the article body on long posts, reinforcing its secondary, supportive role.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element fixed within the left sidebar column; it scrolls away with the page and does not stick, preserving the simple, clean layout of the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The CTF metadata panel is the topmost element in the left sidebar column under the hero. It begins at the same vertical position as the top of the article body, creating a clean horizontal alignment across the two columns. The panel spans the full sidebar width and is styled as a compact card with 16–24px internal padding and possibly a subtle outline or top accent line. Inside, key challenge metadata (difficulty, category, platform, flags, etc.) is organized in short labeled rows or a simple key-value grid, with 8–12px vertical spacing between lines. There is 24–32px of whitespace between this block and the hero above, matching the spacing between hero and the main article column, reinforcing the shared structure. The bottom of the panel has 16–24px of space before the recommended tools block starts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content block anchored to the top of the left sidebar; it scrolls with the page and maintains its relative vertical alignment with the article body.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The series part information appears at the very top of the right article column, just below the hero and above the main article content. It is a slim, horizontal breadcrumb-like bar that spans the full width of the article column, with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px left and right padding (matching the article body text inset). The text is left-aligned and might read, for example, “Part 2 of 5 in ‘Web Exploitation Series’,” possibly with subtle links to previous/next parts. It is separated from the hero by a vertical gap of 24–32px, and from the start of the article body by 16–24px, acting as a gentle, unobtrusive preface to the content. The styling is understated (slightly smaller text, muted color), keeping it visible but not overpowering the article title or hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline block at the top of the right column; it scrolls naturally with the article content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comments section is anchored below the article body within the right main column. After the end of the article content, there is a clear vertical separation of about 32–48px, followed by a “Comments” heading left-aligned to match the article text. The comments list spans the full width of the article column (minus its inner padding) and consists of individual comment blocks stacked vertically. Each comment block has 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px vertical spacing from the next, with subtle separators such as thin bottom borders or slight background alternation, but no heavy card shadows. Avatars, author names, timestamps, and comment text are arranged in a clear hierarchy, with the comment text flowing similarly to article paragraphs for readability. The entire comments list stays within the main column width and does not overlap the sidebar area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the right column; increases the scroll height and maintains the editorial stacking pattern (article → comments → feedback).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comment form is positioned immediately below the comments list within the right main column, separated by 24–32px of vertical whitespace. It uses the same width as the article content, maintaining consistent left and right alignment. The form is presented as a simple, flat block with 16–24px padding and minimal borders. A small heading or label (“Add a comment”) is left-aligned at the top, followed by input fields stacked vertically: a multi-line text area for the comment body and, if needed, name/email fields, each with 8–16px spacing between them. The submit button sits at the bottom, right-aligned relative to the form’s inner width, with 16–24px spacing above it. The overall height is modest, encouraging quick interaction without feeling cluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the bottom of the comments area; it expands vertically as needed and scrolls with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback section appears as the final block in the right main column, below the comment form. It is separated from the comment form by a 32–40px gap, signaling a new interaction type. The block spans the same width as the article and comment content and is styled as a lightweight panel with 16–24px internal padding. A concise, left-aligned prompt like “Was this article helpful?” appears at the top, followed by simple controls (e.g., thumbs up/down, a brief ratings scale, or a tiny text input) arranged horizontally or vertically with 8–12px spacing. The section is intentionally compact in height so it does not visually compete with comments or the article body. There is generous whitespace (32–48px) below this final block before the end of the page or footer starts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static end-of-page section within the right column; it scrolls with the page and acts as a soft terminus for the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search form is embedded in the fixed top navigation bar, aligned to the right side of the nav’s internal content container. The navigation bar spans the full width of the viewport with a height of roughly 56–64px. Inside, the search form presents as a single, horizontally oriented input field (and optional icon) about 240–320px wide, vertically centered within the nav. A 16–24px gap separates the search form from the right edge of the nav container, and there is adequate space (24–32px) between the search and any adjacent nav links or profile controls, preserving the minimal, uncluttered look. The search input has subtle styling: a thin border, slightly darker background than the nav, and no heavy shadows. On smaller screens, the search may compress to an icon or shorten in width but remains anchored to the top-right, above the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed within the top navigation bar, which stays pinned to the top of the viewport while the rest of the content (hero, article, sidebar) scrolls beneath it.

name: Series List
title: Series
description: Page for browsing, filtering, sorting, and paginating multi-part learning series, with navigation to individual series detail pages.

core_features: Load available topic and sort options via getSeriesFilterOptions and render them as interactive filters.
Read current URL parameters (topic, sort, page) to determine the active filter, sort mode, and page index for the series list.
Fetch a paginated list of series from getSeriesList using the current topic, sort, and page state, and display them as clickable series cards.
Provide interactive topic and sort controls that update the URL parameters and reload the series list without relying on local storage.
Provide pagination controls that update the page URL parameter and reload the series list while preserving the current topic and sort state.
Allow navigation from a series card to the corresponding series_detail.html page by passing the selected series identifier as a URL parameter.
Optionally allow navigation directly to article.html for the first part of a series from a call-to-action on the series card when the corresponding article identifier is available in the loaded data.
user_workflows: Browse all series with default settings: 1) User opens series.html without URL parameters. 2) The page calls getSeriesFilterOptions to load available topic and sort options. 3) The page derives topic, sort, and page from the URL (all missing, so no topic filter and no explicit sort are applied). 4) The page calls getSeriesList with topic and sort left undefined and page set to a valid default (e.g., 1). 5) The list of series returned by getSeriesList is displayed; the user can click any series card to open series_detail.html for that series.

Filter series by topic: 1) While on series.html, the user opens the topic selector in the filters bar. 2) The user chooses a topic value provided by getSeriesFilterOptions.topicOptions. 3) The filters component updates the URL so that topic is set to the selected value, sort stays unchanged (if present), and page is reset to 1. 4) The URL change triggers a new call to getSeriesList using the selected topic, the current sort (if any), and page=1. 5) The list updates to show only series whose topic matches the selected filter.

Change series sorting: 1) While viewing the list, the user opens the sort selector, which is populated using getSeriesFilterOptions.availableSortOptions. 2) The user selects a sort option (for example, one of the values returned by the API, such as a ‘most_popular’-like option or a ‘newest’-like option). 3) The sort selection updates the URL so that sort is set to the selected value, topic remains unchanged (if present), and page is reset to 1. 4) The page calls getSeriesList again using the current topic, the newly selected sort, and page=1. 5) The list re-renders according to the chosen sort order.

Navigate between pages of series: 1) The user is viewing a list of series with topic and sort already determined by the URL. 2) Pagination controls are rendered based on getSeriesList.totalCount, getSeriesList.page, and getSeriesList.pageSize. 3) The user clicks a page number, or a next/previous control. 4) The pagination component updates the URL page parameter to the chosen page number while preserving the current topic and sort. 5) The page calls getSeriesList with the same topic and sort but the new page value. 6) The visible series list updates to correspond to the selected page.

Open a series overview page from a card: 1) The user finds a series of interest in the list. 2) The user clicks on the series card. 3) The click handler reads the series identifier from the series object returned by getSeriesList (for example, using the slug field). 4) The browser navigates to series_detail.html with the URL parameter seriesId set to the selected series identifier. 5) The user is now on the series_detail.html page for that series.

Optionally start reading Part 1 directly: 1) The user finds a series and sees a 'Start Part 1' style call-to-action rendered on the card only when the series data returned by getSeriesList includes a first-part article identifier. 2) The user clicks this call-to-action. 3) The click handler reads the corresponding articleId from the series item data. 4) The browser navigates to article.html with articleId set to that dynamic value. 5) The user is now on the article.html page for the first part of the series.
interactions: On initial page load, the page reads topic, sort, and page from the URL query string and normalizes them (e.g., converting page to a positive integer, and treating absent values as undefined).
On initial page load, the page calls getSeriesFilterOptions with no parameters to fetch the available topicOptions and availableSortOptions; these are then bound to the topic and sort UI controls.

After filter options are loaded and URL state is known, the page calls getSeriesList with parameters topic (from URL or undefined), sort (from URL or undefined), page (from URL or a default valid page), and pageSize (from the application’s configuration), then binds the returned series, totalCount, page, and pageSize to the list and pagination components.

When the user changes the topic filter, the filters component updates the URL parameter topic to the newly selected value, clears or leaves sort as-is depending on current state, sets page=1, and triggers a re-fetch via getSeriesList using the new URL state.

When the user changes the sort option, the filters component updates the URL parameter sort to the newly selected value, leaves topic unchanged, sets page=1, and triggers a re-fetch via getSeriesList using the new URL state.

When the user clears topic or sort (for example, choosing an 'all' or unfiltered state represented by one of the values provided by getSeriesFilterOptions), the component updates the URL to remove or reset that parameter and reloads the list with getSeriesList using the resulting URL state.

When the user uses pagination controls, the pagination component updates the page parameter in the URL while preserving existing topic and sort parameters; it then triggers a call to getSeriesList with the updated page value.

When the user clicks a series card, the click event determines the series identifier from the bound data (for example, the slug for that series) and navigates the browser to series_detail.html?seriesId=<derivedSeriesId>.

When the user clicks a 'Start Part 1' call-to-action on a card that has an associated first-part article identifier in its data, the event handler navigates the browser to article.html?articleId=<derivedArticleId>.

When the URL changes (for example, via browser back/forward navigation, or when filters/pagination are adjusted), the state manager logic re-parses the topic, sort, and page parameters, and re-runs getSeriesList using those values to keep visible data synchronized with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully controlled by URL query parameters and API responses, without using localStorage. On load, the page reads topic, sort, and page from the URL search parameters. If page is absent or not a valid positive integer, the state manager treats it as 1 for API calls and can normalize the URL accordingly when the user interacts again. If topic or sort are absent, they are passed as undefined to getSeriesList so that the backend can apply its own default behavior. The getSeriesFilterOptions call is performed independently of URL state and always returns the full set of topicOptions and availableSortOptions. The currently selected topic and sort in the UI are derived by matching URL.topic and URL.sort against the values returned by getSeriesFilterOptions; if a URL value does not match any returned option, the control behaves as if no selection is active and can optionally update the URL when the user makes a new valid selection. When the user changes topic or sort, the new values are immediately encoded into the URL query string (series.html?topic=<value>&sort=<value>&page=1), or the corresponding parameter is removed/reset if the user chooses an 'unfiltered' option. When the user changes page using the pagination controls, only the page parameter is updated, preserving topic and sort as-is. Any URL parameter change that affects topic, sort, or page triggers a fresh call to getSeriesList with those parameters plus a pageSize determined by application configuration. The getSeriesList response (totalCount, page, pageSize, series[]) then drives the list rendering and pagination range; the current page indicator in pagination is bound to getSeriesList.page rather than the URL alone, ensuring the UI reflects the actual data returned. Navigation to series_detail.html and article.html is purely via query parameters derived from the currently selected series or its first-part article (when available) and does not modify any state in localStorage.

components.functionality: Manages synchronization between the URL query parameters (topic, sort, page) and the internal state used for calling getSeriesList. On initialization, it parses the URL, normalizes parameter values (e.g., ensuring page is a positive integer), and exposes the effective topic, sort, and page values to other components. It listens for browser navigation events (back/forward) and internal requests to update topic, sort, or page, updates the URL accordingly, and triggers re-fetching of series data when the effective state changes.

Provides topic and sort controls for the series list. On load, it uses getSeriesFilterOptions to populate the available topic and sort options. It reconciles the current selections with the URL-derived topic and sort values. When the user selects or clears a topic or sort option, it does not call getSeriesList directly; instead, it sends a change request to the URL state manager, which updates the URL and causes the list to be re-fetched.

Fetches and displays a paginated list of series according to the current topic, sort, and page state. After the URL state manager provides effective values for topic, sort, and page, it calls getSeriesList with those values and a pageSize from application configuration. It renders one series-card component per item in the getSeriesList.series array, and it displays an appropriate empty state when totalCount is zero.

Represents a single series entry in the list. It displays the series title, description, topic, and any other metadata provided in each Series item from getSeriesList.series, such as total_parts, estimated_total_time_minutes, popularity_score, and timestamps, without hardcoding any specific values. The entire card (or a dedicated action area) is clickable to navigate to series_detail.html with a seriesId parameter derived from the series data (for example, the slug field). If the series data includes a first-part article identifier, the card may render an additional 'Start Part 1' style call-to-action that navigates to article.html with that articleId.

Renders page navigation controls based on getSeriesList.totalCount, getSeriesList.page, and getSeriesList.pageSize. It calculates the available page numbers from these values and highlights the current page according to getSeriesList.page. When the user chooses a different page (via a numbered button or next/previous controls), it does not directly call getSeriesList; instead, it sends a page change request to the URL state manager, which updates the URL page parameter and triggers getSeriesList with the new page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid similar to Bootstrap: a full-width container for the hero and navigation, then a centered content container with a ~3/9 or 4/8 column split between sidebar and main content.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: fixed top navigation (global), followed by a full-width dark hero introducing the 'Learning Series' context, then a centered two-column content area. In that main area, the left column continues to house author/identity elements (from the original mockup), while the right column is dedicated to the learning series list. Within the right column, the sequence is: section heading (e.g., 'All Series'), then the series-filters-bar running horizontally across the top of the column, followed by the vertically stacked series-list-container (holding individual series-card items), and finally the series-pagination at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: fixed top navigation (global), followed by a full-width dark hero introducing the 'Learning Series' context, then a centered two-column content area. In that main area, the left column continues to house author/identity elements (from the original mockup), while the right column is dedicated to the learning series list. Within the right column, the sequence is: section heading (e.g., 'All Series'), then the series-filters-bar running horizontally across the top of the column, followed by the vertically stacked series-list-container (holding individual series-card items), and finally the series-pagination at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies the top 25–30% of the viewport height and spans 100% width. Below it, the main content is in a centered container at roughly 70–80% of viewport width with 10–15% gutters on either side. Inside the container, the left sidebar uses about 25–30% of the width, while the right column (where all series-related components live) uses 70–75%. Within that right column, the filters bar and pagination controls span the full width; series cards are full-width items stacked vertically with 24–32px gaps. Vertical section separations follow the 32–48px rhythm: hero → content container, series heading → filters bar, filters bar → list, list → pagination.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page uses a moderately spacious editorial density. Text lines are kept to comfortable lengths via the central container. Each series-card has 16–24px internal padding, with 8–16px vertical spacing between text elements (title, meta, excerpt). Series items are separated primarily by whitespace (24–32px) instead of heavy borders. The filters bar is compact but still airy, with controls spaced by 8–16px, and pagination is light and unobtrusive. Overall, the page favors readability and scannability over fitting maximum cards above the fold.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s Z-pattern. On load, the eye hits the top-left logo, scans across the nav, then diagonally down to the hero title and description on the left. From there, it moves down into the right-column main content where the section heading and filters bar form the first horizontal scan line, then continues vertically down the series list. The left sidebar remains a secondary, occasional glance zone, producing a subtle F-pattern down the page. Within the main column, the flow is: heading → filters bar (left-to-right) → first series-card → subsequent series-cards → pagination at the bottom.

layout.overall_description: The Series List page appears as a clean, minimalist cybersecurity blog view with a slightly corporate tone. A fixed, full-width navigation bar sits at the very top, with the site logo aligned left and simple text links aligned right. Directly below, a dark, moody full-width hero band spans the screen, occupying roughly the top quarter of the viewport; its background subtly suggests code or a cybersecurity motif, while a large, left-aligned heading introduces the learning series (e.g., 'Learning Paths' or 'Deep Dive Series'), accompanied by a short description. Beneath the hero, the content contracts into a centered container that leaves generous gutters on both sides. This container is split into two columns: a narrower left sidebar holding the author identity and supporting info, and a wider right column that functions as the main series browser. At the top of this right column sits a simple section label like 'All Series' or 'Browse Series', echoing the 'Recent Posts' label from the mockup, with a subtle horizontal rule or hairline beneath. Immediately below, a horizontal filters bar spans the width of the right column, aligning neatly with the heading above. Below the filters bar, the main series-list-container stacks a vertical sequence of flat, unobtrusive series cards, each representing a learning series with a bold title and short description, separated by consistent whitespace rather than heavy lines. At the bottom of this column, centered pagination controls offer page navigation while remaining visually light. The entire layout feels orderly and calm, guiding the eye from top navigation to hero, then down into filtered, scannable content in a way that aligns with a professional, content-first hacking blog.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The series-url-state-manager has no visible footprint on the page; it lives conceptually at the top of the main content area, just beneath the hero in the DOM hierarchy, but does not occupy any rendered space. It ties together the filters, list, and pagination components by synchronizing their state with the browser URL (query parameters for page number, active filters, sort options, etc.). Visually, nothing is drawn for this component—no borders, no background—so it does not disturb the clean, minimalist layout. Its presence is purely behavioral, ensuring that when users adjust filters or change pages, the URL reflects this and the same view can be restored on reload or sharing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual logic-only component; it does not affect layout dimensions or flow. It should be treated as a background controller that listens to and updates other visible components without rendering UI or consuming spacing in the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The series-filters-bar is positioned within the right main column, directly under the section heading (e.g., 'All Series') and any subtle horizontal divider. It spans the full width of this column, which itself takes up roughly 70–75% of the centered container. The bar forms a low-profile horizontal strip with 16–24px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside its own bounds. Controls (search input, dropdowns, pill toggles) are aligned from left to right, starting at the left edge of the main column, visually forming the first interactive scan line after the heading. Adjacent controls are separated by 8–16px gaps, keeping the bar airy and uncluttered. There is a modest 16–24px vertical gap between the heading above and the top of the filters bar, and another 16–24px gap between the bottom edge of the filters bar and the first series-card below. The bar uses a flat style: a very light background tint or a subtle bottom hairline to distinguish it from the content area, avoiding heavy borders or shadows, in line with the minimalist editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: By default, it scrolls with the page as part of the right column. Optionally, on larger screens it can become sticky within the viewport relative to the main content area (pinning just beneath the global nav/hero once scrolled), but if so, it should do this without drop-shadows—only a subtle divider line at the bottom to indicate separation when fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The series-list-container sits immediately below the series-filters-bar in the right main column and occupies the full width of that column. It acts as a vertical stack region for individual series-card components. The top of the container begins about 16–24px below the bottom edge of the filters bar, aligning its left edge exactly with the left edge of the filters bar and section heading to maintain a strong columnar rhythm. Each child series-card is stacked one after another with 24–32px vertical spacing between them, using whitespace rather than solid dividers to separate items. The container itself does not have a visible border; instead, its bounds are implied by the consistent alignment with the right column and the pagination controls that appear 32–40px below the final card. horizontally, the container’s width is constrained by the main content column, which takes up roughly 70–75% of the central page width, leaving generous gutters between the cards and the browser edges, supporting comfortable reading lengths.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It participates in the normal vertical document flow, expanding in height as more series-card items are rendered and pushing the pagination controls downward. There is no fixed or floating behavior; it simply scrolls with the page as a cohesive list area within the right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each series-card appears as a flat, rectangular content block within the series-list-container, spanning nearly the full width of the right main column. It maintains 16–24px of padding on all sides, echoing the padding found in the blog’s post previews. At the top of the card, a bold, left-aligned series title anchors the eye, roughly matching the weight and size of post titles in the mockup. Directly beneath, there may be a compact metadata row (e.g., number of parts, difficulty level, estimated duration) rendered in a smaller, muted type; this row is separated from the title by about 8–12px of vertical space. Below that, a short series description or excerpt flows across the width of the card, with a line length tuned by the column width and a comfortable line height for reading. Optional tag pills or a subtle 'View Series' affordance can appear at the bottom, separated by another 8–12px. Vertically, within the card, each text block is spaced by 8–16px, creating a structured but relaxed rhythm. There is no heavy border or drop shadow; the card may use a very light background tint or a 1px hairline border in a low-contrast color to distinguish it from the page background. The entire card behaves as a single clickable area, with a subtle hover treatment such as a slight background shade shift or a thin accent-colour border, staying within the flat aesthetic. Between adjacent cards, there is 24–32px of vertical whitespace, so each series-card reads as an individual editorial unit, similar to blog post previews.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Series cards are static blocks in the vertical flow. They do not fix or float; instead, they stretch naturally to fit their content. On smaller screens, each card continues to span the full available width of the single-column layout, maintaining its internal padding and vertical spacing while collapsing any multi-column internal layouts (e.g., meta and tags) into a simple vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The series-pagination component is positioned at the bottom of the right main column, directly beneath the series-list-container. It begins approximately 32–40px below the last series-card, creating a clear visual separation between content and navigation. The pagination controls are horizontally centered within the main column, not full-bleed to the browser edges. They form a low-profile horizontal row of elements: a 'Previous' control on the left, a compact sequence of page numbers in the center, and a 'Next' control on the right, all aligned on a single baseline. Each control has 8–12px horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding, with 8–10px gaps between individual page numbers, preserving the page’s airy feel. The typography is modest and slightly lighter than primary headings, signaling secondary importance. There might be a very subtle top hairline or slightly increased top margin to visually separate pagination from the main list without introducing heavy bars. Below the pagination, an additional 48–64px of bottom padding before any footer maintains the open, breathing room characteristic of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The pagination is part of the normal document flow and scrolls with the rest of the page. It does not stick or float; it simply marks the logical end of the series list. When interacting (changing pages), it works together with the series-url-state-manager to update the visible portion of the series-list-container while preserving the same layout and scroll position pattern.

name: Series Overview
title: Series Overview
description: Displays an overview of a selected multi-part series with metadata and an ordered list of all parts, and provides clear starting points and navigation to each article.

core_features: Load and display series-level information using getSeriesDetails based on the seriesId URL parameter.
Show series metadata including title, description, topic, total parts, estimated total reading time, and timestamps.
Render an ordered list of all series parts using the parts array from getSeriesDetails, showing part number, title, and reading time for each.
Provide navigation from each part entry to its corresponding article detail page via article.html?articleId=...
Highlight a recommended starting point (e.g., the first part in the series) so users know where to begin.
Offer navigation back to the Series List (series.html) via a breadcrumb or back link.
Handle loading, missing seriesId, or API error states without using any hardcoded data.
user_workflows: View a series overview: 1) User navigates to series_detail.html?seriesId=... from index.html, series.html, or article.html. 2) On page load, the series-detail-controller reads seriesId from the URL. 3) The controller calls getSeriesDetails({ seriesId }). 4) While waiting, a loading state is shown. 5) On success, the controller passes the series data to the header, recommendation, and parts list components, which render series metadata and the ordered parts list.

Start the series from the recommended first part: 1) After series details load, the series-start-here component inspects the parts array and identifies the part with the lowest seriesPartNumber as the recommended starting point. 2) The component displays a “Start from Part X” action linked to article.html?articleId=<articleId of that part>. 3) User clicks the action. 4) Browser navigates to article.html with the selected articleId in the query string.

Jump directly to a specific part: 1) User scrolls to the parts list. 2) User locates a desired part in the ordered list (e.g., by part number, title, or reading time). 3) User clicks the part title. 4) Browser navigates to article.html?articleId=<clicked part’s articleId>. 5) If the user uses Ctrl+click or middle-click, the part opens in a new tab as a standard browser behavior.

Return to the Series List: 1) User wants to see other series. 2) User clicks the breadcrumb/back link rendered by the series-breadcrumb component. 3) Browser navigates to series.html (no additional parameters required).

Handle invalid or missing seriesId: 1) User lands on series_detail.html without a seriesId, or with an invalid seriesId. 2) On initialization, the series-detail-controller detects that seriesId is missing or calls getSeriesDetails and receives an error. 3) The controller displays an error/empty state explaining that the series could not be loaded and provides a link back to series.html via the series-empty-state component.
interactions: Initial data load: On page load, the series-detail-controller reads the seriesId from the query string and triggers getSeriesDetails. The user does not manually submit a form; the load is automatic.

Click on a series part title: When the user clicks a part title within the series-parts-list component, the click handler builds a URL of the form article.html?articleId=<part.articleId> and navigates the browser to that URL in the current tab (normal click) or new tab (Ctrl+click / middle-click, handled by the browser).

Click on the recommended starting part: When the user clicks the primary action in the series-start-here component, the click handler navigates to article.html?articleId=<articleId of the recommended starting part>.
Click breadcrumb/back link: When the user clicks the breadcrumb/back link in the series-breadcrumb component, the browser navigates to series.html, allowing the user to browse other series.
Error/empty state interaction: If there is no seriesId or getSeriesDetails fails, the series-empty-state component displays a message and a link. Clicking this link navigates to series.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies on the seriesId URL parameter in the query string (series_detail.html?seriesId=...). On initialization, the series-detail-controller parses the current URL to extract seriesId. If seriesId is present, the controller calls getSeriesDetails({ seriesId }) and stores the returned object as the current in-memory page state, including series metadata and the parts array. The series-metadata-header, series-start-here, and series-parts-list components all bind to this shared state. The ordered list of parts is determined by the seriesPartNumber value from the parts array; if needed, parts are sorted in ascending seriesPartNumber in memory rather than via any hardcoded order. If seriesId is missing, the controller does not call getSeriesDetails and instead sets a 'missingSeriesId' state flag, which causes the series-empty-state component to render an appropriate message with a link back to series.html. If getSeriesDetails returns an error, an 'error' state flag is set, and the series-empty-state component displays an error message. No series or article data is hardcoded; all displayed series titles, descriptions, topics, counts, and times are taken directly from the getSeriesDetails response for the current seriesId. Navigation to article.html always passes the clicked part's articleId via the URL query string (article.html?articleId=...), and navigation back to the Series List always targets series.html without relying on localStorage or any client-side persistence beyond the current URL.

components.functionality: Central controller that reads the seriesId from the URL, invokes getSeriesDetails, manages loading/error state, and provides series data to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Triggered when the page loads. Reads seriesId from window.location.search. If present, calls getSeriesDetails({ seriesId }). Sets internal state to 'loading' until the promise resolves.

onSeriesDetailsLoaded: Called when getSeriesDetails resolves successfully. Stores the returned series object (including properties such as title, description, topic, totalParts, estimatedTotalTimeMinutes, createdAt, updatedAt, and parts). Clears 'loading' state and 'error' flags.
onSeriesDetailsError: Called when getSeriesDetails fails (e.g., network or not found). Stores an error flag/state and clears 'loading'.
onUrlChange: (Optional in SPA contexts) Triggered when the URL changes to a different seriesId while remaining on this page. Re-runs the onInit logic with the new seriesId.

components.functionality: Provides a simple breadcrumb or back link that lets users navigate back to the Series List page (series.html).
event_handlers: onClickBackToSeriesList: Triggered when the user clicks the breadcrumb/back link. Executes a navigation to series.html (e.g., window.location.href = 'series.html').

components.functionality: Displays key metadata about the current series so users understand the overall topic, scope, and effort before starting.
event_handlers: onSeriesDataUpdate: Triggered when series-detail-controller provides or updates series data. Binds and displays fields from the getSeriesDetails response, including: title, description, topic, totalParts, estimatedTotalTimeMinutes, createdAt, and updatedAt. No data is hardcoded; all content is sourced from the API response.

components.functionality: Highlights the recommended starting point in the series (typically the earliest part) and provides a direct action to open it.
event_handlers: onSeriesDataUpdate: Triggered when series-detail-controller passes the series data. The component inspects the parts array returned by getSeriesDetails and identifies the part with the lowest seriesPartNumber as the recommended starting part. It does not assume any static part number; it uses only the dynamic data.

onClickStartHere: Triggered when the user clicks the primary 'start' action. Reads the articleId of the recommended starting part and navigates to article.html?articleId=<recommendedPart.articleId> using browser navigation (e.g., window.location.href).

components.functionality: Renders an ordered list of all parts in the series with their part numbers, titles, and reading times, allowing navigation to each article.
event_handlers: onSeriesDataUpdate: Triggered when the series-detail-controller provides the parts array from getSeriesDetails. The component sorts parts by seriesPartNumber in ascending order if needed and renders each part row using dynamic values: seriesPartNumber, title, readingTimeMinutes, slug (if used in display), and publishedAt.

onClickPartTitle: Triggered when a user clicks a part title in the list. Reads that part's articleId and navigates to article.html?articleId=<part.articleId> using standard anchor link behavior. The component does not hardcode any articleId; it uses the value provided in the parts array.
onClickPartRow: (Optional) If the UX allows clicking the entire row, this handler mirrors onClickPartTitle and navigates to article.html?articleId=<part.articleId>.

components.functionality: Handles missing-seriesId, empty-parts, and error states by showing an appropriate dynamic message and a link back to the Series List.
event_handlers: onStateChange: Triggered when the series-detail-controller updates its state flags for 'missing seriesId', 'loading', 'error', or 'loaded'. When 'missing seriesId' or 'error' is true, the component displays a non-hardcoded, generic error message (e.g., based on state) and a link back to series.html. When the series is loaded but parts is an empty array, it displays an empty-series message indicating that no parts are available yet.
onClickBackToSeriesListFromEmpty: Triggered when the user clicks the back/Series List link inside the empty or error message. Navigates to series.html.

components.functionality: Shows a loading state while series details are being fetched from getSeriesDetails.
event_handlers: onLoadingStateChange: Triggered by the series-detail-controller when the 'loading' flag changes. When loading is true (after onInit and before onSeriesDetailsLoaded/onSeriesDetailsError), the indicator is shown. When loading is false, the indicator is hidden. No series or article data is hardcoded; this component only reflects the loading state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid similar to Bootstrap: a full-width container for the hero and navigation, then a centered content container with a ~3/9 or 4/8 column split between sidebar and main content.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing fixed top navigation and full-width dark hero, the Series Overview page uses the same centered two-column layout from the mockup. The left column remains the global sidebar (author card + contact list). The right column is the series-focused content stack: at the very top of this column sits the series breadcrumb, followed immediately by the series metadata header. Below that, a prominent 'Start Here' recommendation panel spans the width of the main column, followed by either the ordered list of series parts or, if no parts are available, the empty state message. When data is loading, the loading indicator appears in the main column where the list/message would normally sit. The data-loader controller conceptually wraps this whole main-content column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing fixed top navigation and full-width dark hero, the Series Overview page uses the same centered two-column layout from the mockup. The left column remains the global sidebar (author card + contact list). The right column is the series-focused content stack: at the very top of this column sits the series breadcrumb, followed immediately by the series metadata header.
