Below that, a prominent 'Start Here' recommendation panel spans the width of the main column, followed by either the ordered list of series parts or, if no parts are available, the empty state message. When data is loading, the loading indicator appears in the main column where the list/message would normally sit. The data-loader controller conceptually wraps this whole main-content column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero continues to occupy roughly the top 25–30% of the initial viewport at full width. Beneath it, the primary content container is centered with generous left and right gutters (~10–15% of viewport width on large screens). Inside this container, the left sidebar occupies about 25–30% of the width, and the right main column with series content uses the remaining 70–75%. Within the main column, the breadcrumb is a slim horizontal strip at the top, the metadata header takes up the first substantial block of vertical space, the Start Here panel is a medium-height card directly under it, and the parts list expands vertically as needed. Vertical separations follow the system: 24–32px between major blocks (breadcrumb → metadata → start-here → parts list), and 16–24px between elements within each block.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a moderately spacious editorial density. Text lines are limited to a comfortable measure in the main column with generous line-height. Components are stacked vertically with clear breathing room, relying on whitespace rather than heavy borders. The metadata header and Start Here panel have slightly tighter internal grouping to keep related details cohesive, while the series parts list uses consistent vertical spacing between items (around 24px) to make scanning easy without feeling crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow replicates the mockup’s Z-pattern and F-pattern. The eye moves from the logo and navigation at the top left across the nav, then diagonally down into the hero’s left-aligned title, and then into the main content area. In the main container, the eye lands first on the series breadcrumb at the top of the right column, then drops to the larger, bolder series metadata header. From there, attention moves to the Start Here recommendation panel (a visually emphasized full-width card), and then continues downward through the vertically ordered list of series parts. The left sidebar remains a secondary F-pattern column, occasionally drawing the eye for author/contact context but never overpowering the series content on the right.

layout.overall_description: After the fixed horizontal navigation and full-width, dark, moody hero banner, the Series Overview content sits inside a centered, max-width container with wide side gutters. This container is split into a narrower left sidebar and a wider right column. The sidebar continues the site-wide pattern, showing the author card and contact links. The right column is the focus: at its top edge, just below the hero’s bottom margin, a slim breadcrumb row grounds the user in the site structure. Directly under it, a bold, left-aligned series metadata header acts as the main entry point into the series, mirroring the visual role of the ‘Recent Posts’ heading in the mockup. Beneath that, a prominent but clean ‘Start Here’ recommendation panel spans the width of the main column, clearly indicating the best entry article. Below this panel, a subtle horizontal divider or spacing break leads into a vertically stacked, ordered list of all series parts, each occupying the full width of the main column. If there are no parts, a centered empty-state message replaces the list in that same space. While data is being fetched, a minimalist loading indicator appears in the main column where the list would be, maintaining the structural rhythm. The overall effect is a calm, editorial layout where the series title and recommended starting point are immediately visible, and the list of parts flows naturally downward within a balanced two-column page structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The series-detail-controller is not a visible UI element but conceptually wraps the entire series content area in the right main column. Spatially, it corresponds to the vertical slice starting just below the hero’s bottom edge and extending down to the end of the series-related content, within the 70–75% width main column on the right. All other series components inside this main column (breadcrumb, metadata header, start-here panel, parts list, empty state, loading indicator) are children of this controller in a linear top-to-bottom flow. It does not occupy its own visual height or add extra spacing; instead, it governs which of the child components are visible within this fixed structural slot.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an invisible container tied to the main content column’s normal document flow. It scrolls with the page and does not apply any special fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The series-breadcrumb sits at the very top of the right main content column, directly below the hero section’s lower edge and any top padding of the content container. It is a low-profile horizontal row that spans roughly 100% of the main column’s width but uses modest vertical height—about 24–32px of content plus 8–16px of padding above and below. The breadcrumb text is left-aligned, starting flush with the main column’s left edge (aligned with the series metadata header below). There is approximately 8–12px of vertical space between the bottom of the breadcrumb and the top of the series-metadata-header to keep them visually connected while still distinct. On smaller screens, when the layout collapses to a single column, the breadcrumb remains at the top of the stack, maintaining its position above the metadata header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the normal document flow, scrolling with the page. No sticky behavior; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down the series content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The series-metadata-header is the first visually dominant block in the right main column, placed immediately beneath the series-breadcrumb with a small vertical gap (around 8–12px). It spans the full width of the main column (70–75% of the overall content width) and sits flush with the column’s left and right padding. The header includes a large, bold, left-aligned series title and supporting metadata (e.g., number of parts, estimated reading time, last updated date) arranged in a tight vertical stack with 8–16px between lines. Overall, this block has generous padding of about 16–24px on all sides to give the title room to breathe, and about 24–32px of vertical space separates its bottom edge from the top of the series-start-here panel. There is no heavy bounding box; at most, a subtle bottom rule or a slight tonal difference from the background can be used, maintaining the flat, minimalist feel. As the user scrolls, this header anchors the beginning of the series content, much like a section heading in an editorial layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element in the main column, participating in the normal vertical flow. It does not stick to the top; it scrolls off-screen as the user moves down through the series parts.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The series-start-here recommendation panel sits directly beneath the series-metadata-header, separated by a clear vertical gap of approximately 24px to mark it as a distinct yet closely related section. It spans the full width of the right main column and is styled as a medium-height, rectangular card with 16–24px padding on all sides. Its content is left-aligned and vertically stacked: a small, all-caps or label-style heading such as 'Start here' at the top, followed by a brief explanatory sentence, and then a highlighted link or button to the recommended entry article. The card may use a slightly darker or tinted background than the page body to subtly elevate its importance without heavy shadows or borders, consistent with the minimalist aesthetic. There is a moderate vertical space (24–32px) between the bottom of this panel and the top of the series-parts-list, so that the Start Here recommendation reads as a featured gateway before the detailed list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width card within the main column’s flow. It scrolls naturally with the rest of the content and does not overlap or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The series-parts-list occupies the main vertical body of the right column beneath the series-start-here panel. It begins after a vertical margin of roughly 24–32px, optionally introduced by a subtle section label such as 'All parts in this series' and/or a thin horizontal rule that echoes the 'Recent Posts' divider from the mockup. The list content is arranged as a vertical stack of full-width list items, each representing one part of the series. Each item has 16–24px padding, a bold, left-aligned title at the top, and a shorter, lighter-weight excerpt or brief description below, separated by 8–12px of vertical spacing. Items are separated from each other by 24px of whitespace, with no heavy borders—at most, a very light divider line or subtle change in background on hover. The list itself stretches downward as far as needed, maintaining consistent left alignment with the metadata header and Start Here panel. On smaller screens, the list simply narrows with the single-column layout and maintains the same vertical stacking and spacing rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically expanding section within the main column. It scrolls with the page and does not have sticky headers or floating controls by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The series-empty-state occupies the same spatial slot as the series-parts-list, only appearing when there are no parts to show. It is placed directly beneath the series-start-here panel or, if the start-here panel is not relevant in an empty scenario, directly beneath the series-metadata-header, maintaining the 24–32px vertical separation from whichever component precedes it. The message is contained in a centered, full-width block within the main column, using 16–24px padding and a modest max-width on the text itself so it doesn’t stretch too wide. The content is left-aligned but visually centered in terms of attention, with a concise headline (e.g., 'No parts available yet') and a short explanatory sentence below, separated by 8–12px. Additional guidance (such as a link back to all series or to the blog home) can appear below with slightly more spacing. There are generous vertical margins (32–48px) above and below this block to give the empty state a calm, intentional feel instead of a cramped error message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that replaces the series-parts-list in the normal flow. It does not overlay other components; it simply occupies their space when triggered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The series-loading-indicator appears in the right main column in place of the series-parts-list (and, if appropriate, before the Start Here panel’s content is fully available) while data is being fetched. Vertically, it sits below the series-metadata-header, respecting the same 24–32px gap that the Start Here panel or parts list would use, or directly under the Start Here panel if the recommendation is known before the full list loads. The indicator is horizontally centered within the main column but constrained to a small, unobtrusive area—such as a spinner icon or subtle progress bar accompanied by a short 'Loading series…' label. Surrounding the loading indicator is ample whitespace (at least 32px above and below) to keep it from feeling cramped, with no heavy container or card. As content loads, this indicator disappears and the normal components (Start Here panel, parts list or empty state) take over the same spatial region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Transient inline element in the normal flow. It does not fix to the viewport; it scrolls with the rest of the content, and is shown or hidden by the series-detail-controller based on loading state.

name: Reading List & Collections
title: Reading List & Collections
description: Page for viewing and managing saved articles in the default Reading List and other custom collections, including switching collections, updating reading status, and basic collection management.

core_features: Load and display all available collections with their names, descriptions, item counts, and default-reading-list flag using getCollectionsOverview.
Determine the active collection based on the collectionId URL parameter or, if absent/invalid, the default Reading List from getCollectionsOverview.

For the active collection, fetch and display its saved articles with metadata (title, category, reading time, date added, reading status, etc.) using getReadingListArticles for the default list or getCollectionItems for other collections.
Allow switching between collections (e.g., via sidebar or tabs); switching updates the collectionId in the URL and reloads the active collection view.
Allow opening any saved article in its detail page by clicking its title, navigating to article.html with the appropriate articleId parameter.
Allow updating an article’s reading status (not_started, in_progress, completed) directly from the list using setArticleReadingStatus.
Allow removing an article from the currently active collection using removeArticleFromCollection, updating the displayed list and collection item count.
Allow adding an article from the current list to another existing collection via an inline control using addArticleToCollection, without leaving the page.
Allow renaming non-default collections using renameCollection, updating both the sidebar/tabs and the active collection header.
Allow deleting non-default collections using deleteCollection and then redirecting the user to the default Reading List collection by updating the collectionId URL parameter.
Provide optional client-side filtering/sorting controls (e.g., by reading status, date added, reading time) operating on the currently loaded items without calling additional interfaces.
user_workflows: View default Reading List workflow: 1) User navigates to reading_list.html without a collectionId parameter (e.g., via header/footer navigation). 2) Page controller calls getCollectionsOverview to load all collections. 3) Page logic selects the collection where isDefaultReadingList is true as the active collection. 4) Page controller calls getReadingListArticles to retrieve items for that collection. 5) Collections sidebar/tabs render all collections using the overview data, marking the default collection as active. 6) Collection items list renders all retrieved articles with their metadata and controls for status, removal, and adding to other collections.

View a specific collection workflow: 1) User arrives at reading_list.html with a collectionId parameter from another page or by clicking a collection in the sidebar/tabs. 2) Page controller calls getCollectionsOverview. 3) Page logic checks if the URL collectionId exists in the overview data; if it does, that collection becomes active. 4) If the active collection is the default reading list (isDefaultReadingList is true), the controller calls getReadingListArticles; otherwise it calls getCollectionItems with the URL collectionId. 5) Sidebar/tabs highlight the active collection; collection header shows the active collection’s name and description. 6) Collection items list displays the retrieved items for that collection.

Switch collections from within the page workflow: 1) User clicks a collection entry in the sidebar/tabs. 2) Collection switcher event handler updates the browser location to reading_list.html with collectionId set to the selected collection’s ID. 3) Page reloads and reruns the initial load logic (getCollectionsOverview + getReadingListArticles or getCollectionItems depending on the active collection). 4) Active collection and items update to reflect the selected collection.

Update reading status from list workflow: 1) In the active collection list, the user interacts with a reading-status control (e.g., dropdown or segmented toggle) for a specific article. 2) Control’s change handler calls setArticleReadingStatus with the relevant articleId and selected readingStatus. 3) On success, the component updates the local representation of that item’s readingStatus in the collection items list. 4) Any client-side filters or progress indicators that depend on readingStatus refresh using the updated value.

Remove article from collection workflow: 1) In the active collection list, the user clicks a remove action for a specific article. 2) Optionally, a confirmation interaction is shown client-side (no additional interface call). 3) On confirmation, the handler calls removeArticleFromCollection with the active collectionId and articleId. 4) On success, the article is removed from the in-memory list of items for that collection, and the remainingItems value from the response can be used to update the displayed item count for that collection. 5) If the list becomes empty, the component displays an appropriate empty-state message without assuming any static text content beyond generic messaging.

Add article to another collection workflow: 1) In the active collection’s list, the user opens an inline control (e.g., dropdown) to select another collection for the article. 2) The control uses collection data from getCollectionsOverview to list available target collections, excluding or including the current one based on client logic. 3) When the user selects a target collection, the handler calls addArticleToCollection with the current articleId and chosen collectionId. 4) On success, the UI can provide a transient success indication and, if needed, update any local counts for the target collection. The article remains visible in the current collection unless the user explicitly removes it using the removal control.

Rename collection workflow: 1) With a non-default collection active (or selected via sidebar options), the user triggers a rename action (e.g., menu item or button). 2) A rename dialog opens with its input prefilled from the collection’s current name. 3) The user submits the new name via the dialog. 4) Dialog submit handler calls renameCollection with the active collectionId and newName. 5) On success, the sidebar/tabs update the collection’s displayed name, the active collection header updates, and the dialog closes. 6) The URL collectionId does not change, ensuring that bookmarks to this collection remain valid.

Delete collection workflow: 1) With a non-default collection selected, the user triggers a delete action. 2) A confirmation dialog shows the collection’s current name to confirm deletion (no extra interface). 3) On confirmation, the dialog handler calls deleteCollection with the selected collectionId. 4) On success, the deleted collection is removed from the in-memory list of collections obtained from getCollectionsOverview. 5) The page logic determines the default Reading List (isDefaultReadingList true) from the remaining collections and sets it as the next active collection. 6) The browser navigates to reading_list.html with collectionId set to this default collection’s ID (or without a parameter if the implementation prefers to rely on default-selection logic). 7) The page reloads, calls getReadingListArticles, and displays the default Reading List.

Open article from collection workflow: 1) In the collection items list, the user clicks on an article title or designated open action. 2) The click handler constructs a navigation URL to article.html with articleId set to the article’s identifier. 3) The browser navigates to article.html, where the article detail page handles its own loading using the provided articleId.
interactions: Clicking a collection entry in the sidebar/tabs triggers navigation to reading_list.html with the selected collectionId URL parameter, causing the active collection and items to reload based on that ID.
Clicking the site logo or a header link designated for the homepage triggers navigation to index.html without additional parameters.
Clicking an article title within the collection items list navigates to article.html with articleId set to that item’s articleId.
Changing the reading status control for an article triggers a call to setArticleReadingStatus with the articleId and selected readingStatus, then updates the UI to reflect the new status.

Clicking a remove control for an article triggers optional client-side confirmation and then a call to removeArticleFromCollection with the active collectionId and that article’s articleId; on success, the article row is removed from the list.

Using an 'add to collection' control for a given article triggers a call to addArticleToCollection with the chosen target collectionId and the articleId; on success, the UI can show a non-blocking success indicator and adjust local item counts for the target collection.

Opening the rename collection dialog (e.g., via a button or menu) for a non-default collection allows the user to change the collection name; submitting the form triggers renameCollection and, on success, updates all occurrences of the collection name in the sidebar/tabs and header.

Opening the delete collection dialog for a non-default collection allows the user to confirm deletion; confirming triggers deleteCollection, and on success the page redirects the user to the default Reading List collection by updating the collectionId parameter in the URL.

Optional filter or sort controls above the items list (e.g., by readingStatus, addedAt, readingTimeMinutes) adjust which items are visible and in what order purely in client state without calling additional interfaces.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by the collectionId URL parameter and the data returned from getCollectionsOverview. On initial load, the page reads collectionId from the query string. It then calls getCollectionsOverview to obtain all collections and identify which one, if any, has isDefaultReadingList set to true. If the URL collectionId matches an existing collection from this overview, that collection becomes the active collection. If the URL collectionId is missing or does not match any known collection, the default collection (where isDefaultReadingList is true) becomes active. If no default can be identified from the overview, the implementation may fall back to the first collection returned, but this remains within client logic and does not require new interfaces. Once the active collection is determined, the page decides which retrieval interface to call: if the active collection is marked as the default reading list, it calls getReadingListArticles; otherwise, it calls getCollectionItems with the active collectionId. All per-article interactions (status change, add to collection, remove from collection) and collection management actions (rename, delete) operate on this in-memory state and do not alter the URL except where the active collection changes (e.g., switching collections or redirecting after a delete). When switching collections, the handler updates the collectionId query parameter and reloads the page, ensuring that the current state can be bookmarked or shared via the URL. The page does not use localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms; all persistent state changes rely solely on the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, active collection selection, and high-level state management for reading_list.html. On load, reads collectionId from the URL, calls getCollectionsOverview, determines the active collection (either matching the URL parameter or the default reading list), then calls getReadingListArticles or getCollectionItems accordingly. Exposes the resolved collections list and active collection items to child components and handles page-level navigation updates when the active collection is changed.

Displays all collections in a sidebar or tabbed interface using data from getCollectionsOverview. Highlights the active collection determined by the page controller. Provides actions to switch to another collection by navigating to reading_list.html with the corresponding collectionId in the URL. Exposes optional actions (e.g., context menu or buttons) for renaming and deleting non-default collections, delegating to the appropriate dialogs.

Shows metadata for the currently active collection, including its name, optional description, and item count derived from the active items list or from getCollectionsOverview. For non-default collections, exposes controls to open the rename and delete dialogs. For the default reading list, these controls are disabled or hidden according to client logic based on isDefaultReadingList.

Renders the list of articles belonging to the active collection, using items from getReadingListArticles or getCollectionItems. Each row displays article metadata such as title, categoryName, readingTimeMinutes, addedAt, publishedAt, upvotes, and readingStatus. Each row wires into further controls for opening the article, updating reading status, removing from the collection, and adding to other collections. Supports optional client-side filtering and sorting based on the currently loaded items.

Attached to each article row (e.g., on the title); when activated, navigates to article.html with articleId corresponding to that item’s articleId. Does not call additional interfaces; navigation is performed purely through URL changes.

Per-article control embedded in each collection item row that displays the current readingStatus and allows the user to change it (e.g., between not_started, in_progress, completed). On user change, calls setArticleReadingStatus. On successful response, updates the associated item’s readingStatus in the collection-items-list.

Per-article control that removes the article from the currently active collection. When clicked and optionally confirmed, it calls removeArticleFromCollection with the active collectionId and the articleId. On success, it updates the collection-items-list by removing the item row and uses remainingItems from the response to adjust the displayed item count for the active collection.

Per-article control (e.g., dropdown, menu, or dialog trigger) that lets the user add the article to another existing collection. It reads available collections from the data already loaded by getCollectionsOverview and allows the user to select a target collection. On selection, it calls addArticleToCollection with the articleId and chosen collectionId. On success, it may show a transient success indication and update local item counts for the destination collection in the sidebar.

Dialog that allows the user to rename a selected non-default collection. It displays the current collection name as the initial value in an input field. When the form is submitted, it calls renameCollection with the selected collectionId and the newName entered by the user. On success, it updates the affected collection’s name in the collections-sidebar and active-collection-header, then closes the dialog.

Dialog for confirming deletion of a non-default collection. It shows the current collection name for context. On user confirmation, it calls deleteCollection with the selected collectionId. On success, it removes that collection from the in-memory collections list, asks the page controller to determine the default reading list collection, and triggers navigation to reading_list.html with the default collection’s ID (or no collectionId) so that the user is returned to a valid collection view.

Optional controls displayed above the collection-items-list that allow the user to filter and sort the currently loaded items (for example by readingStatus, addedAt, readingTimeMinutes, or upvotes). These operations are applied purely to the in-memory list returned by getReadingListArticles or getCollectionItems and do not invoke additional interfaces.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid similar to Bootstrap: a full-width container for the hero and navigation, then a centered content container with a ~3/9 or 4/8 column split between sidebar and main content.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear flow: fixed global navigation at the very top (outside this scope), then a full‑width hero band for the 'Reading List & Collections' page title, and below that a centered main content container with a two‑column layout. The left column becomes the collections sidebar (switching between default Reading List and custom collections). The right column becomes the main working area: active collection header at the top, then a compact filter/sort bar, followed by a vertical list of saved articles. Each article row groups its controls (open link, status toggle, add/remove) in a single horizontal strip, with the title and main metadata dominating the left side and action controls aligned to the right.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear flow: fixed global navigation at the very top (outside this scope), then a full‑width hero band for the 'Reading List & Collections' page title, and below that a centered main content container with a two‑column layout. The left column becomes the collections sidebar (switching between default Reading List and custom collections). The right column becomes the main working area: active collection header at the top, then a compact filter/sort bar, followed by a vertical list of saved articles. Each article row groups its controls (open link, status toggle, add/remove) in a single horizontal strip, with the title and main metadata dominating the left side and action controls aligned to the right.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Hero is full‑width and approximately the top 25–30% of the initial viewport height, with content left‑aligned inside a max‑width container and generous vertical padding (32–48px). Below the hero, a centered main container spans about 70–80% of the viewport width, leaving 10–15% gutters on each side on desktop. Inside this container, the left sidebar takes roughly 25–30% of the width, and the right main area takes 70–75%. Vertical spacing between major blocks (hero → main container, sidebar modules, header → filter bar → list) is in the 24–48px range, while inner spacing (between text lines and controls) uses 8–16px increments.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density matches a modern editorial layout: generous line height, clear breathing room around headings, and article rows tall enough to be easily scannable without feeling crowded. Collection lists in the sidebar are compact but not crammed, with 8–12px between items. Article rows use whitespace rather than heavy dividers: 16–24px internal padding, subtle separators or just gap space between rows. Controls are grouped but not stacked too tightly, making them easy to hit and read while preserving a clean, minimalist feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The primary flow preserves the Z‑pattern: users start at the top left with the site logo in the nav, skim across nav links, then drop down diagonally to the large hero title on the left. From there, the eye moves into the main container, catching the active collection header at the top of the right column, then the filter/sort bar, and then scanning down through the list of articles. The collections sidebar on the left forms a secondary F‑pattern: the eye periodically shifts left to scan down the collection names, then back right to see the corresponding articles. Within each article row, the flow runs left‑to‑right: title and metadata first, then reading status, then add/remove actions toward the far right edge.

layout.overall_description: The page appears as a clean, minimalist cybersecurity reading hub that closely mirrors the original blog layout. A fixed horizontal navigation sits at the very top, with a dark, moody full‑width hero just beneath it. The hero uses a dark background with subtle imagery suggesting hacking or code, and a large, left‑aligned heading like 'Reading List & Collections' that anchors the page. Below the hero, the content snaps into a centered two‑column grid inside a max‑width container: on the left, a tidy collections sidebar listing the default Reading List and custom collections; on the right, a wider main area showing the active collection name, a concise summary (item counts, status), and a slim filter/sort bar. Under that, saved articles are presented as a vertical stack of flat, border‑light rows with clear whitespace and readable typography. Each row lets users open the article, toggle its reading state, and manage its collection membership. When renaming or deleting a collection, a simple, centered modal overlays the page with a dimmed background, maintaining the minimalist, slightly corporate aesthetic and keeping focus squarely on the task at hand.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller spans the full available width from the left to right edge of the viewport and orchestrates the layout from directly below the fixed top navigation down to the page footer. It first renders a full‑width hero section occupying roughly the top quarter of the viewport height, with its content constrained to a central max‑width (approximately 1100–1200px). Immediately below the hero, it places a centered main content container with wide left and right gutters (about 10–15% of viewport width each) and uses a 12‑column grid to split the space into a narrower left sidebar (about 3–4 grid columns) and a wider right main area (about 8–9 grid columns). Inside this grid, the collections sidebar is anchored to the left column, while the active collection header, items filter/sort bar, and the collection items list stack vertically in the right column. The controller also mounts the collection rename and delete dialogs as fixed overlays above all other content when needed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container that scrolls with the page; manages overall section stacking (hero, main two‑column layout, overlays) without its own visual chrome.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collections sidebar occupies the entire left column within the main content container, starting at the same vertical position as the top of the active collection header on the right. It is approximately 25–30% of the content width on desktop, with 16–24px internal padding and a flat, card‑like background that either matches the main page background or is subtly elevated using a very light tint or hairline border. At the top of the sidebar, a small heading such as 'Collections' is aligned to the left, with about 16px of space below it before the list of collection entries begins. Each collection entry is a single horizontal row spanning almost the full sidebar width, with 8–12px vertical spacing between rows and 8–16px left padding to allow for an optional indicator or icon. The active collection is visually highlighted (e.g., bolder text or a subtle background strip) but maintains the minimalist aesthetic. The sidebar extends downward as far as its content requires and aligns vertically with the article list, so the left and right columns create a cohesive two‑column band under the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page as part of the main content. On taller viewports it can optionally become sticky relative to the top of the main container (starting a bit below the hero), so the collection list remains visible while scrolling through articles.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active collection header sits at the very top of the right main column, horizontally aligned with the top of the collections sidebar. It spans the full width of the right column (about 70–75% of the content width) and uses 16–24px padding on all sides. The collection name is left‑aligned as the primary text, with a medium‑to‑large font size that stands out but is still subordinate to the page hero title. Directly beneath the title, there is 4–8px of vertical space, followed by a subtle line of metadata (e.g., '12 items • 5 unread') in a smaller, muted type. Optionally, a small set of collection‑level actions (such as a slim 'Rename' text button) can be aligned to the right on the same baseline as the collection title or metadata, keeping the header relatively low in height (around 56–80px total). Below the header, a 16–24px vertical gap leads into the items filter and sort bar, creating a clear but compact separation between identification and controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the scrolling main column; acts as the top anchor for the right column content and is displaced upward as the user scrolls down the article list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collection items list occupies the entire remaining vertical space of the right main column below the filter and sort bar. It spans the full width of that column and arranges saved articles as a vertical stack of uniform rows or light cards. Each article row is a flat rectangle with 16–24px padding on all sides and extends nearly edge‑to‑edge within the column, with 12–16px of vertical gap between consecutive rows. A subtle divider (hairline or low‑contrast line) may appear at the bottom of each row or between rows, but whitespace remains the primary separator. Within each row, the left two‑thirds of the width are reserved for article information and the primary open link, while the right third is reserved for controls such as the reading status toggle and add/remove buttons. This arrangement keeps the list visually clean and scannable, with titles aligned nicely along a left vertical axis and action icons/buttons forming a right‑aligned column down the page. As the user scrolls, the list extends downwards, with the layout maintaining consistent vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrollable content area within the right column; its height is unconstrained so it grows with the number of articles. No internal fixed elements; relies on the surrounding page scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within each article row in the collection items list, the article open link is the primary text element. It is placed at the top left of the row, occupying the full width of the left content area (approximately the left two‑thirds of the row). The article title is set in a bold or semi‑bold style, with comfortable line height and 0–4px of space above and 4–8px of space below before any secondary metadata (such as source or estimated reading time). The entire title text is clickable, and optionally the left portion of the row can be treated as a click target for opening the article, while the right‑aligned controls remain separate. This left‑anchored, text‑dominant positioning mirrors the emphasis on blog post titles in the original mockup and ensures the open link visually leads each row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard inline/inline‑block within the article row’s content area; participates in wrapping and vertical stacking with metadata and does not float or fix.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading status control appears within each article row in the same horizontal band as the title and primary actions, but slightly separated to emphasize its secondary nature. It is typically positioned just to the right of the text block or aligned toward the right side of the row, depending on available width. On wider screens, it can sit between the article info (left) and the add/remove buttons (far right), creating a three‑segment layout: title/metadata on the left, status in the middle, management controls on the right. The control itself is compact—a pill, toggle, or small button with a concise label such as 'Unread' or 'Done'—and maintains at least 8–12px of padding from surrounding elements. Vertically, it aligns with the baseline of the title or slightly below, ensuring a tidy, linear reading flow while keeping the control quickly accessible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline or inline‑flex element inside the row’s horizontal layout that aligns itself between text and action buttons; scrolls normally with the list with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove‑from‑collection control is positioned at the far right edge of each article row, sharing a horizontal line with the reading status control and potentially the add‑to‑collection control. It appears as a small, low‑emphasis button or icon button (e.g., an 'x' or trash icon) that is right‑aligned with 8–16px right padding from the row’s edge. To maintain the minimalist aesthetic, its styling is subdued—light outline or muted text color—becoming more prominent on hover. Vertically, it is centered within the row’s primary content area, aligning with the title’s baseline or slightly lower if needed. This far‑right placement makes it easy to quickly remove items while reviewing the list, without distracting from the main reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed position relative to its article row, right‑aligned within that row’s control area; it scrolls with the list and does not change position independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add‑to‑collection control sits immediately to the left of, or occasionally just above, the remove‑from‑collection control in the right side of each article row, forming a compact cluster of management actions. It may appear as a small button with an icon (e.g., '+') and label like 'Add to…'. The control is right‑aligned within the same vertical slice as the remove button, with 8–12px of horizontal spacing between them to avoid crowding. When activated, it can open a small inline popover or external panel (not detailed here), but in its resting state, it occupies only a modest footprint to keep the row visually clean. Its vertical alignment matches that of the reading status control, so all interactive elements create a neat horizontal band on the right half of the row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right‑aligned inline‑flex element that sits next to the remove control inside each article row; scrolls normally with the list and does not overlay other elements by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collection rename dialog appears centered in the viewport as a modal overlay when triggered from the active collection header or sidebar. It floats above a semi‑transparent dark backdrop that covers the entire page, slightly dimming the hero and content below. The dialog itself is a simple rectangular panel with a flat, light background, roughly 480–560px wide on desktop and with 24–32px padding on all sides. At the top of the panel, a concise title such as 'Rename Collection' is left‑aligned, with 16–24px of space below it before the input field. The text input spans nearly the full width of the modal content area, with 8–16px spacing between the label (if present) and the field. Below the field, the primary and secondary action buttons (e.g., 'Save' and 'Cancel') are right‑aligned in a single horizontal row with 8–12px between them and 16–24px of padding below before the bottom edge. The dialog does not use heavy shadows—just a soft, minimal elevation or border consistent with the overall design DNA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the center of the viewport with a full‑screen backdrop; does not scroll with the underlying content. If the content exceeds viewport height on small screens, the modal may scroll internally while remaining vertically centered relative to the visible area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collection delete dialog is also a centered modal overlay with a semi‑transparent dark backdrop, but slightly more compact than the rename dialog to reflect its concise, confirmatory nature. It is a flat rectangular panel approximately 400–480px wide with 24px padding. A clear, left‑aligned warning title like 'Delete Collection?' appears at the top, followed by 8–16px of vertical space and a short paragraph explaining the consequences (e.g., 'This will remove this collection but not delete the articles themselves.'). Below the message, the action buttons are right‑aligned in a single row, with the destructive action (e.g., 'Delete') visually emphasized and placed to the far right, and a safer 'Cancel' button to its left, separated by about 8–12px. Overall height is modest, and the dialog’s simple layout keeps the user’s focus on the decision at hand.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed, centered modal overlay identical in behavior to the rename dialog: it overlays a full‑screen backdrop and does not move with the underlying page scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The items filter and sort bar is positioned directly beneath the active collection header in the right main column, separated by 16–24px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the column and is relatively shallow—about 40–56px in height—with 8–16px internal padding. The bar follows a left‑to‑right arrangement: a search or filter input sits on the left, occupying roughly 40–60% of the bar’s width, while one or two compact sort and status filter controls are grouped on the right. The right‑aligned controls might include a sort dropdown and a simple chip or dropdown for filtering by reading state (e.g., All, Unread, Done). The bar’s background either matches the main page background or uses a very subtle tint to visually separate it from the list, with no heavy border, maybe just a fine bottom border line that aligns with the minimalist aesthetic. A 16–24px gap separates the bottom of the bar from the first article row, ensuring the bar reads as a distinct control strip before the list begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, scrolling offscreen as the user scrolls down the article list. On very tall desktops, it could optionally become sticky to the top edge of the main column once scrolled, but by default it behaves as a normal block element.

name: Settings
title: Settings
description: Configure personal settings for the blog, with a focus on topic-based notification preferences such as delivery type and frequency per topic.

core_features: Tabbed settings layout that uses the URL query parameter `tab` to determine and persist the active settings section, including a Notifications section.
Automatic loading of all current notification settings via `getNotificationSettings` when the Notifications section is active.
Topic-based notifications table where each row represents a topic returned by `getNotificationSettings` and provides controls to edit delivery type and frequency.
Per-topic control of notification delivery type (e.g., on-site alerts, email, disabled) using editable form controls bound to each `NotificationSetting` record.
Per-topic control of notification frequency (e.g., instant, daily, weekly, none) with UI logic that enforces consistent combinations with the selected delivery type.
Ability to effectively disable notifications for specific topics by selecting a disabled/off delivery type, which forces frequency to a non-sending state.
Save Changes action that collects all modified notification topics and calls `updateNotificationSettings` to persist the new preferences in bulk.
Feedback on save requests, including success confirmation and error messaging, driven by the response from `updateNotificationSettings`.
Deep-linking and in-page navigation for the Notifications section using `settings.html?tab=notifications` when the user clicks the Notifications option in the settings sidebar.
Navigation back to the main index page when the user clicks the site logo or Home in the header while on the Settings page.
user_workflows: Open Notifications Settings via navigation: 1) User clicks the Settings link in the header or footer. 2) Browser navigates to `settings.html` (optionally with a `tab` parameter). 3) On load, the page reads `tab` from the URL. 4) If `tab=notifications` or no tab is provided, the Notifications section becomes the active tab. 5) The page calls `getNotificationSettings` once the Notifications tab is active. 6) The returned list of notification settings is displayed as a table of topics, each with editable delivery type and frequency.

Deep-link directly to Notifications: 1) From elsewhere on the site (e.g., index or internal links), the user follows a link to `settings.html?tab=notifications`. 2) On load, the page parses `tab=notifications` and immediately activates the Notifications section. 3) The page calls `getNotificationSettings` and populates the table with current settings. 4) The active state of the Notifications option in the sidebar is visually indicated based on the `tab` parameter.

Adjust notification preferences for a single topic: 1) In the Notifications table, the user locates a row for a topic (for example, a topic related to OSINT or malware). 2) The user changes the delivery type control (e.g., from on-site alerts to email, or to a disabled/off option). 3) When the user selects a disabled/off delivery type, the component automatically sets that topic’s frequency value to the non-sending option (e.g., `none`) and optionally disables or pre-selects the frequency control so no sending frequency is used. 4) When the user selects a non-disabled delivery type, the frequency control is enabled and the user can select a sending frequency (instant, daily, or weekly). 5) The component marks this row as modified in local state. 6) The Save Changes button becomes enabled when at least one row has unsaved modifications.

Adjust notification preferences for multiple topics at once: 1) The user edits delivery type and frequency in several topic rows using the same controls and logic as for a single topic. 2) Each edit updates the in-memory representation of the corresponding `NotificationSetting` and flags that row as modified. 3) The Save Changes button reflects the presence of unsaved changes. 4) The user can navigate between topics and continue editing without leaving the Notifications tab.

Save updated notification settings: 1) After making changes, the user clicks the Save Changes button at the bottom of the Notifications section. 2) The click handler gathers only the `NotificationSetting` records that have been modified, including their identifiers and updated delivery type and frequency values. 3) The page calls `updateNotificationSettings` with a `settings` parameter containing this list of modified settings. 4) While the request is in progress, the Save Changes button enters a loading state to prevent duplicate submissions. 5) If `updateNotificationSettings` returns success, the page updates the in-memory notification settings list using `updatedSettings` from the response, clears the modified flags, resets the loading state, and shows a success confirmation. 6) If the call fails or returns `success=false`, the page shows an error message without discarding the user’s unsaved changes, allowing the user to retry after adjusting as needed.

Navigate between settings tabs while preserving Notifications state: 1) While on the Notifications tab, the user may click another settings option in the sidebar. 2) The click updates the `tab` parameter in the URL to the new tab value and navigates there. 3) If the user navigates back to the Notifications tab (e.g., `settings.html?tab=notifications`), the page reads the `tab` parameter and reactivates the Notifications section. 4) If the implementation re-initializes, `getNotificationSettings` is called again to refresh the table; otherwise, the latest in-memory settings from the previous visit may be reused as long as they are consistent with the current state.

Return to Home from Settings: 1) At any time on the Settings page, the user clicks the site logo or a Home link in the header. 2) The page performs a navigation to `index.html` without additional parameters. 3) Any unsaved changes in the Notifications section are discarded unless the user explicitly chooses to save them before navigating away (an optional confirmation can be shown before leaving if unsaved changes are detected).
interactions: Click on the Notifications option in the settings sidebar: Updates the URL to `settings.html?tab=notifications` and activates the Notifications section. If the Notifications data has not yet been loaded in the current session, this triggers a call to `getNotificationSettings`.

Page initialization on load or navigation: Reads the `tab` query parameter from the URL. If the parameter equals `notifications` or no parameter is provided (depending on the chosen default behavior), sets the active tab to Notifications and calls `getNotificationSettings`. If another tab is active, the Notifications section and its data may remain dormant until the user navigates to it.

Change delivery type for a topic row: User changes a select or similar control bound to the `delivery_type` field of a specific `NotificationSetting`. This updates the local copy of the setting and may trigger validation rules, such as automatically setting `frequency` to `none` when `delivery_type` is `disabled`, or enabling/disabling the frequency control as appropriate.

Change notification frequency for a topic row: User changes a select or similar control bound to the `frequency` field of a `NotificationSetting`. The control only allows valid values (instant, daily, weekly, or none). If the selected delivery type is disabled, the frequency control is either locked to `none` or otherwise constrained to prevent sending frequencies. Any change marks that row as modified in local state.

Save Changes button click: When clicked, the handler collects all locally modified `NotificationSetting` objects, constructs a `settings` array for `updateNotificationSettings`, and calls the interface. During the call, the UI enters a saving state (e.g., disabling the button, showing a spinner). Once a response is received, the UI either updates the local data with `updatedSettings` and shows a success message or displays an error and allows the user to attempt saving again.

Handling `updateNotificationSettings` response: On success (`success=true`), the handler replaces or merges the existing local `NotificationSetting` records with those returned in `updatedSettings`, clears modified flags, and may display the message returned from the API. On failure, the handler keeps user changes intact, surfaces the returned message as an error, and leaves the Save Changes button enabled for retries.

Error handling during `getNotificationSettings`: If `getNotificationSettings` fails, the Notifications table shows an error state and a retry action. Clicking retry will call `getNotificationSettings` again. Until a successful response is received, the table remains empty or in an error state.

Click on the site logo or Home link in the header while on Settings: Triggers navigation to `index.html` without changing notification settings. If the implementation includes unsaved-changes protection, a confirmation can appear before navigation if any `NotificationSetting` rows are marked as modified.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Settings page uses the `tab` URL query parameter to determine and persist the active settings section. On each page load or navigation within the app, the script parses `tab` from `settings.html?tab=...`. If `tab=notifications` or if the implementation chooses Notifications as the default when `tab` is missing, the Notifications tab is marked active and the notifications-specific components are initialized. When the Notifications tab becomes active for the first time during a session, the page calls `getNotificationSettings` and stores the resulting array of `NotificationSetting` objects in local state (for example, `notificationSettings`). Each entry in `notificationSettings` represents a topic and includes fields such as `id`, `topic`, `display_name`, `delivery_type`, and `frequency`. Local UI state also maintains derived flags like `isLoadingNotifications`, `notificationsLoadError`, `isSavingNotifications`, and `hasUnsavedChanges`. Whenever the user changes delivery type or frequency for a topic, the corresponding `NotificationSetting` object in local state is updated and that topic is marked as modified (e.g., by tracking its `id` in a set of dirty IDs). Validation logic ensures that when `delivery_type` is set to `disabled`, `frequency` is automatically set to `none` and the frequency control is constrained so that only non-sending behavior is possible. Conversely, if `delivery_type` is changed from `disabled` to another value, the component ensures that `frequency` is set to a valid sending frequency (instant, daily, or weekly) if appropriate. The Save Changes button’s enabled/disabled state is derived from whether any rows are marked as modified and whether a save operation is currently in progress. When the user clicks Save Changes, the component compiles a `settings` array containing only the modified `NotificationSetting` objects, then calls `updateNotificationSettings(settings)`. While awaiting the response, `isSavingNotifications` is true and the Save Changes button is disabled to prevent duplicate submissions. On a successful response, the component updates the local `notificationSettings` array from `updatedSettings`, clears the dirty set, sets `hasUnsavedChanges` to false, and resets `isSavingNotifications` to false. On failure, it leaves the modified values intact, sets an error state (e.g., `notificationsSaveError`), and allows the user to retry. Navigation between tabs updates the `tab` query parameter in the URL, either by full navigation or via history APIs; on each such change, the page re-evaluates which components to render based on the new active tab. Navigation to other pages like `index.html` clears the in-memory state, and any unsaved changes are lost unless explicitly saved beforehand.

components.functionality: Provides the overall structure of the Settings page, including the header (with logo/Home navigation), sidebar for selecting different settings sections, and main content area that renders the currently active tab such as the Notifications section.

Renders navigation options for the available settings sections. Highlights the currently active section based on the `tab` query parameter and triggers navigation to `settings.html?tab=notifications` when the Notifications item is clicked.

Acts as the main container for the Notifications settings. It becomes visible when the active tab (from the URL) is `notifications`. On first activation, it requests the current notification settings from `getNotificationSettings` and passes them to child components for display and editing.

Handles the lifecycle of loading notification settings. Calls `getNotificationSettings` when the Notifications section is activated, manages loading, success, and error states, and stores the resulting array of `NotificationSetting` records in local state for use by the table component.

Displays the list of notification topics returned by `getNotificationSettings` as rows, with each row exposing editable controls for delivery type and frequency based on the fields of each `NotificationSetting`. Tracks which rows have been modified and propagates changes back to shared state so the Save Changes component can act on them.

Represents an individual topic row within the Notifications table. Binds to a single `NotificationSetting` record, showing the topic display name and providing controls for `delivery_type` and `frequency`. Enforces field-level rules such as synchronizing frequency with delivery type (e.g., setting frequency to `none` when delivery type is disabled) and communicates all changes to the parent table component.

Sits at the bottom of the Notifications section and provides a Save Changes button along with optional status messages. Observes the notifications state to determine whether there are unsaved changes and whether a save is currently in progress. When invoked, it collects the modified `NotificationSetting` objects and calls `updateNotificationSettings` with them, then handles success and error responses.

Displays inline feedback related to the Notifications data lifecycle, including loading indicators from `getNotificationSettings`, error messages if loading or saving fails, and success confirmations when `updateNotificationSettings` completes successfully.

Provides navigation controls in the header for the Settings page, including the site logo and a Home link that navigate back to `index.html`. These interactions do not call any interfaces themselves but affect overall navigation and can optionally be constrained by unsaved-changes checks maintained by the Notifications components.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid similar to Bootstrap: a full-width container for the hero and navigation, then a centered content container with a ~3/9 or 4/8 column split between sidebar and main content.
layout.layout_pattern: The Settings page keeps the same macro structure as the blog mockup: a full-width, fixed header navigation at the very top; directly beneath it, a full-width, low-height hero band with a dark, moody background and a left-aligned page title like “Settings / Notifications”; below the hero, a centered main container that uses a two-column layout. The left column becomes a vertical settings sidebar (tab-navigation) in place of the author card. The right, wider column holds the active settings panel—in this case the notifications section—with a heading area, status messages, and then a vertically stacked notification settings table. A bottom save bar overlays the lower edge of the main container when there are unsaved changes.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The Settings page keeps the same macro structure as the blog mockup: a full-width, fixed header navigation at the very top; directly beneath it, a full-width, low-height hero band with a dark, moody background and a left-aligned page title like “Settings / Notifications”; below the hero, a centered main container that uses a two-column layout. The left column becomes a vertical settings sidebar (tab-navigation) in place of the author card. The right, wider column holds the active settings panel—in this case the notifications section—with a heading area, status messages, and then a vertically stacked notification settings table. A bottom save bar overlays the lower edge of the main container when there are unsaved changes.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and hero both span 100% of the viewport width. The hero consumes roughly 25% of the initial viewport height, visually lighter than a full landing hero but clearly distinct. Below that, the main content container is centered with generous left and right gutters of around 10–15% of the viewport width. Inside this container, the sidebar occupies about 25–30% width (roughly 3–4 grid columns), and the main notifications content takes the remaining 70–75% (about 8–9 columns). Vertical spacing between main structural sections (hero to main container; sidebar to notifications content; notifications heading to table; table to save bar) is in the 32–48px range, while internal spacing between text elements and rows stays tighter, around 8–16px.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page uses a moderately spacious, editorial density similar to the blog layout: generous line heights for legibility, clear breathing room around headings and controls, and separation of logical groups via whitespace rather than heavy lines. The notifications table is readable rather than compressed: comfortable row height, clear padding in each cell, and only subtle separators. The sidebar items are vertically spaced enough to avoid visual clutter, reflecting the mockup’s clean, minimalist approach.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow remains a Z-pattern at the macro level: the eye starts at the logo on the top left of the fixed header, scans across the navigation, then drops diagonally down to the left-aligned hero title, and from there moves into the main content. Inside the main container, attention is pulled to the right column’s notifications heading and status messages before scanning downward through the table of topics (an F-pattern down the right column). The left sidebar acts as a secondary vertical anchor: once users orient on the notifications content, their gaze periodically returns left to the settings tabs, reinforcing the current context without stealing focus from the primary table and save actions.

layout.overall_description: The Settings page feels like a configuration-focused variation of the main blog layout. A slim, fixed navigation bar runs full-width at the top with the “Jack Hacks” logo on the left and minimal links on the right. Directly underneath, a dark, full-width hero band stretches edge to edge, its height roughly one quarter of the viewport. On the left side of this hero band, a clear, white page title such as “Settings” with a smaller “Notifications” label beneath anchors the user, while the right side remains mostly empty or softly textured, maintaining the moody cybersecurity aesthetic without adding noise. Below the hero, the content snaps into a centered max-width container with generous gutters on both sides, giving a calm, editorial feel. Inside this container, a two-column grid emerges: a narrower left column displays a vertical settings sidebar with tabs for different settings sections; the wider right column contains the active notifications panel. At the top of this panel, a compact heading and short descriptive text are followed by inline status messages when present. Beneath that, a full-width, neatly spaced table lists notification topics in rows, each row offering inline controls for delivery channel and frequency. The table extends downward with consistent spacing between rows, and when there are pending changes, a minimal, sticky save bar appears at the bottom edge of the viewport within the same content width, aligning with the main column, summarizing changes and presenting primary actions. Overall, the layout is quiet, structured, and content-driven, mirroring the mockup’s minimalist blog aesthetic while clearly signaling that this is a focused configuration workspace.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The settings-layout component acts as the page-level frame. It stretches from just below the fixed header nav down to the bottom of the page. At the top of this frame, it reserves space for a full-width hero band (about 25% of viewport height) with a dark background and left-aligned “Settings / Notifications” title area that visually echoes the blog hero. Directly below the hero, the layout introduces a centered main content container constrained to a comfortable max width (around 1120–1200px), with equal left and right gutters occupying roughly 10–15% of the viewport each. Inside this container, settings-layout establishes a two-column grid: the left column (25–30% width) for the settings-sidebar and the right column (70–75% width) for the notifications-section. Vertical spacing between the hero and this container is around 32px, and between the top of the container and its first child components is around 24px, maintaining the airy editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static structural container that defines the vertical stacking order (hero then main content) and the two-column grid. It adapts responsively by maintaining centered alignment and reflowing the two columns into a single column on narrow screens if needed, but otherwise scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The settings-header-nav is a full-width, horizontally oriented bar pinned to the very top of the viewport, above all other content. It has a modest height (roughly 56–64px) and a light background with a subtle 1px bottom border instead of a shadow, matching the mockup’s minimal chrome. On the left side, aligned with the main content’s future left edge, sits the “Jack Hacks” wordmark or logo. To the right, aligned along the same baseline, a row of simple text links (e.g., Blog, About, Settings, Logout) runs horizontally, spaced using 16–24px gaps. The nav content is center-aligned within the same max-width container as the main content, so logo and links visually line up with the columns below, while the bar itself stretches edge to edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the top of the viewport, remaining visible as the user scrolls. The main page content (including hero) begins just below it, with appropriate top padding so that no content sits underneath the nav.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The settings-sidebar occupies the left column within the centered main content container beneath the hero. It begins near the top of this container, aligning vertically with the top of the notifications-section in the right column. The sidebar is presented as a clean vertical stack of tab-like navigation items (e.g., Profile, Security, Notifications), each one a full-width row within the sidebar column. Each item has 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, with 8–12px space between items. The active tab (Notifications) is styled with a subtle accent: slightly darker background or a thin left border line, but still flat and minimal, preserving the mockup’s aesthetic. The sidebar itself does not have a heavy card border; instead, it relies on alignment and whitespace to separate it from the main content. Its column width is around 25–30% of the main container, leaving a visibly wider area to the right for content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable page content, aligned to the left of the main grid. On taller pages, it scrolls along with the rest of the content rather than remaining sticky, keeping behavior predictable and simple.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notifications-section sits in the right-hand, wider column of the two-column grid, starting at the same vertical level as the top of the settings-sidebar. It spans the full width of this column. At the top of this section, there is about 8–16px of internal padding before the main heading, such as “Notification preferences,” which is left-aligned and visually prominent but smaller than the hero title. Immediately below the heading, with about 8px spacing, a short descriptive line explains what can be configured here. Below that and separated by 16–24px of whitespace, the notifications-status-messages area appears when needed, spanning the full width of the column but not encased in a heavy box. The notification-settings-table follows beneath with a 24–32px gap, filling the remainder of the section. Overall, the section feels like a single, vertically flowing content area where heading, feedback, and table are stacked in a calm, ordered sequence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying the main content column and growing vertically as content (the table) expands. It scrolls with the page, while any sticky elements (like the notifications-save-bar) appear over its lower portion when necessary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification-settings-loader is visually anchored to the notifications-section, specifically overlaying the area where the notification-settings-table normally appears. When active, it presents as a lightweight, semi-transparent layer or centered loading indicator inside the right column, roughly matching the table’s footprint. It does not extend into the sidebar; instead, it respects the width of the main column and is horizontally centered within it. Vertical padding above and below the loader content (e.g., spinner and “Loading notification settings…” text) is spacious—around 32–48px—to maintain the clean, uncluttered feel while settings are fetched.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned relative to the notifications-section, covering or replacing the table area while data is being fetched. It does not affect the layout of the sidebar or hero, and disappears smoothly once loading is complete, allowing the table to occupy its space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification-settings-table resides directly below the notifications heading and any status messages within the right column, separated by approximately 24–32px of whitespace. It spans the full width of the main content column, aligning its left edge with the heading text and running flush to the right boundary of the column. The table uses a minimalist style: column headers (e.g., Topic, Delivery Method, Frequency, Status) are in a single header row with slightly bolder text and a subtle bottom border. Each data row has ample height (around 40–48px) with 12–16px horizontal cell padding, ensuring that controls such as dropdowns or toggles don’t feel cramped. Rows are separated either by a very light horizontal line or by enough vertical spacing that they read as distinct, reflecting the mockup’s preference for whitespace over heavy dividers. The table is tall enough to encourage scrolling when many topics exist, and it visually anchors the main content area between the heading above and the save bar that may appear below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element that expands vertically with the number of rows. It scrolls within the page like normal content. On smaller screens, it may allow horizontal scrolling if necessary, but by default it fits within the main column width without overflowing the overall container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each notification-settings-row is a horizontal band within the table, occupying the full width of the table area. The left side of the row contains the topic name, aligned with other row labels, while the middle and right cells hold interactive elements such as dropdowns (for delivery method and frequency) and possibly a toggle or status indicator. Internal padding on all sides (approximately 8–12px) ensures controls do not touch cell edges and that text maintains comfortable breathing room. When a row is in an editable or focused state, its background can subtly shift to a slightly tinted shade to differentiate it, but still without strong borders or shadows, consistent with the flat design language. Vertical spacing between rows remains consistent so that the entire table reads as a rhythm of evenly spaced lines.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a flexible row inside the table, expanding slightly in height if inline editors (e.g., multi-line selections or tooltips) are present. It does not break the table’s grid; instead, it preserves alignment of all columns while allowing interactive controls to appear or change state within its cells.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notifications-save-bar is a horizontal strip aligned with the main content column at the bottom of the viewport. It spans the same max-width as the main container and is horizontally centered so that its left and right edges line up with the notifications-section and sidebar columns above. Visually, it is slimmer than the hero but more prominent than a standard inline message, with a light background and a subtle top border to separate it from the content it overlays. Inside, content is arranged in a two-part layout: on the left, a short line of text such as “You have unsaved changes” is left-aligned; on the right, primary and secondary buttons (e.g., “Discard” and a highlighted “Save changes”) are right-aligned, with 8–16px spacing between them. Vertical padding within the bar is about 12–16px, creating a compact but comfortable action area that feels consistent with the rest of the design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky to the bottom of the viewport when there are unsaved changes, overlaying the lower portion of the main content column while allowing the page behind it to scroll. It remains horizontally centered and constrained to the page’s main max-width container rather than stretching edge to edge on very wide screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notifications-status-messages component sits within the notifications-section, directly below the section heading and its brief description, and above the notification-settings-table. It spans the full width of the main content column but uses the same internal left and right padding as the surrounding text, so it aligns perfectly with the table and heading. When active, it appears as one or more horizontally stacked alert lines (for success, error, or info), each with a small icon and concise text. These messages are separated from the heading by about 16–24px of vertical space, and from the table by roughly the same distance, acting as a visual buffer between the descriptive top and the interactive table. The styling is restrained: subtle background tints and thin borders for emphasis, avoiding heavy blocks that would disrupt the page’s clean flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the notifications-section, appearing and disappearing without affecting the overall grid. When hidden, the space collapses so the table moves up; when visible, it pushes the table down, maintaining a clear, linear reading order from heading to messages to table.

name: About
title: About
description: Displays dynamically loaded information about the blog author, cybersecurity focus, intended audience, and responsible/legally compliant use of the content, with navigation to contact and home pages.

core_features: Load the About page content dynamically via getStaticPageContent(pageCode), including author background, cybersecurity focus, audience guidance, and responsible usage/legal notes.
Render the returned title as the main page heading and use it to set the document title for the browser tab.
Render the returned contentHtml as the main body of the About page, allowing it to contain structured sections (e.g., background, goals, audience, disclaimers) and inline links.
Display the lastUpdated value from getStaticPageContent as a "Last updated" indicator for transparency about content freshness.
Support deep-linking to specific sections inside the About content using a section query parameter in the URL (about.html?section={sectionCode}).
Allow users to navigate to the contact page from within the About page via one or more contact-related links or call-to-action elements.
Allow users to navigate back to the home page (index.html) by clicking the site logo or a "Home" link in the shared header while on the About page.
user_workflows:
View About information from footer:
1. User is on any site page and clicks the "About" link in the footer.
2. Browser navigates to about.html (optionally without query parameters).
3. On page load, the About page determines its pageCode (e.g., from a server-rendered data attribute or configuration).
4. The page calls getStaticPageContent(pageCode).
5. When the response arrives, the page sets the document title to response.title.
6. The page renders response.title as the visible main heading and response.contentHtml as the main content area.
7. The page shows the lastUpdated value, formatted appropriately, near the content (e.g., below the title or at the bottom).

Deep-link to a specific About section (e.g., disclaimers or responsible use):
1. User follows a link from another page that points to about.html with a section query parameter, such as about.html?section={sectionCode}.
